nodes.name: A Christmas Carol
nodes.description: A Christmas Carol is a novella by Charles Dickens, published in 1843, and republished in various editions including one by J. B. Lippincott Company in 1915, illustrated by Arthur Rackham. | A Christmas Carol is a literary work that depicts the transformation of Ebenezer Scrooge and the enduring message of compassion and Christmas spirit.

nodes.name: Charles Dickens
nodes.description: Charles Dickens is the author of A Christmas Carol, written in December 1843, and is known for his social commentary through literature.

nodes.name: Arthur Rackham
nodes.description: Arthur Rackham is the illustrator of the 1915 edition of A Christmas Carol, known for his distinctive artistic style in book illustrations.

nodes.name: J. B. Lippincott Company
nodes.description: J. B. Lippincott Company is the publisher of the 1915 illustrated edition of A Christmas Carol, based in Philadelphia and New York.

nodes.name: Bob Cratchit
nodes.description: Bob Cratchit is a clerk to Ebenezer Scrooge and a central character in A Christmas Carol, father to Peter and Tiny Tim. | Bob Cratchit is Scrooge's clerk, earning fifteen shillings a week, who lives in a modest four-room house with his family and is blessed by the Spirit. | Bob Cratchit is the father of the Cratchit family, who returns home on Christmas Day wearing a threadbare but carefully maintained outfit and carries Tiny Tim on his shoulder. | Bob Cratchit is the father of the Cratchit family who speaks tenderly about his son Tiny Tim and prepares a hot mixture of gin and lemons. | Bob Cratchit is the father who praises the goose and pudding, declaring the pudding the greatest success of his wife since their marriage. | Bob Cratchit is the father of a large family who celebrates Christmas with modest means, serving hot drinks from limited glassware and expressing love for his son Tiny Tim. | Bob Cratchit is a mild-mannered employee of Scrooge who defends Christmas traditions and speaks of a potential job opportunity for his son, Peter. | Bob Cratchit is the father of the Cratchit family, who is shown with his family in their home by the Ghost. | Bob Cratchit is a grieving father who walks with his deceased son, Tiny Tim, and maintains a cheerful demeanor despite his sorrow. | Bob Cratchit is a father who shares a heartfelt story about the kindness of Mr. Scrooge's nephew and reflects on the memory of his son, Tiny Tim. | Bob Cratchit is the kind-hearted father of Tiny Tim and an employee of Scrooge, who expresses joy and gratitude despite his hardships. | Bob Cratchit is the recipient of a large turkey sent anonymously by Scrooge, and is the father of Tiny Tim. | Bob Cratchit is an employee of Scrooge who arrives late to work after a Christmas celebration, but is rewarded with a salary increase and goodwill. | Bob Cratchit is a character who is addressed by Scrooge in a festive context, and is implied to be an employee who is encouraged to improve his diligence.

nodes.name: Peter Cratchit
nodes.description: Peter Cratchit is the son of Bob Cratchit and appears as a character in A Christmas Carol. | Peter Cratchit is Bob's son, who is being considered for a job that would bring in five-and-sixpence weekly, and is imagined by the family as a future man of business. | Peter Cratchit is one of Bob Cratchit's sons, who shakes hands with his father in a gesture of familial bond and respect.

nodes.name: Tiny Tim
nodes.description: Tiny Tim is the youngest son of Bob Cratchit, a cripple whose character symbolizes innocence and the consequences of societal neglect in A Christmas Carol. | Tiny Tim is Bob Cratchit's son, who uses a little crutch and an iron frame to support his limbs, and is carried home by his father on Christmas Day. | Tiny Tim is a young, crippled member of the Cratchit family who is thoughtful and expresses a wish to be remembered on Christmas Day. | Tiny Tim is a young member of the Cratchit family who expresses joy during the meal by beating the table and crying 'Hurrah!' | Tiny Tim is Bob Cratchit's young, physically disabled son who is cherished by his family and whose potential death is foreseen by the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come. | Tiny Tim is a member of the Cratchit family who drinks the toast to Scrooge without emotion, indicating his detachment from the family's bitterness. | Tiny Tim is a member of a poor but happy family who sings a plaintive song about a lost child in the snow, and is observed closely by Scrooge. | Tiny Tim is a sickly child in the Cratchit family whose death has deeply affected the household, causing quiet grief and emotional strain. | Tiny Tim is Bob Cratchit's deceased child, remembered fondly by the family for his lightness and the love his father had for him. | Tiny Tim is Bob Cratchit's deceased young son, remembered fondly by the family for his patience and mild nature. | Tiny Tim is a young, sickly child in the Cratchit family, whose cheerful spirit and eventual fate serve as a moral catalyst in the story. | Tiny Tim is Bob Cratchit's son, referenced as being significantly smaller than the prize turkey Scrooge intends to send. | Tiny Tim is a character who survives due to Scrooge's changed behavior and is regarded as a symbol of hope and goodwill, with Scrooge becoming a second father to him.

nodes.name: Mr. Fezziwig
nodes.description: Mr. Fezziwig is a kind-hearted, jovial old merchant and former employer of Ebenezer Scrooge, featured in memories from the Ghost of Christmas Past. | A former employer of Ebenezer Scrooge, known for his jovial nature and Christmas celebrations.

nodes.name: Fred
nodes.description: Fred is Ebenezer Scrooge's nephew, representing warmth, family, and generosity in contrast to Scrooge's initial miserliness. | Fred is Scrooge's nephew, to whom Scrooge announces his intention to attend dinner and asks for entry. | Fred is Scrooge's nephew who initiates a guessing game about Scrooge's identity and leads the toast to his uncle's health. | Fred is Scrooge's nephew, who is surprised by Scrooge's sudden visit to his home for dinner, and warmly welcomes him.

nodes.name: Ghost of Christmas Past
nodes.description: The Ghost of Christmas Past is a phantom that shows Scrooge scenes from his past, guiding his moral reflection in A Christmas Carol. | A spirit representing the past, characterized by a kind, generous, and hearty nature. | The Ghost of Christmas Past is a supernatural entity that appears to Scrooge, representing his past, with a fluctuating, ethereal form and a cap that symbolizes his own past actions. | The Ghost of Christmas Past is a supernatural entity that shows Scrooge memories from his childhood, particularly linked to the musical tune played on the harp.

nodes.name: Ghost of Christmas Present
nodes.description: The Ghost of Christmas Present is a spirit embodying generosity and joy, showing Scrooge the current state of others during Christmas. | A spirit representing the present, characterized by a kind, generous, and hearty nature. | The Ghost of Christmas Present is a jolly, majestic spirit dressed in a green robe with fur trim, wearing a holly wreath, and carrying a glowing torch; it represents the current state of Christmas and has over 1,800 elder brothers. | The Ghost of Christmas Present is implied to be aware of the collusion between Topper and Scrooge's nephew during the blind man's buff game. | The Ghost of Christmas Present is a supernatural entity that shows Scrooge scenes of current Christmas celebrations, contributing to his emotional awakening.

nodes.name: Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
nodes.description: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is an ominous apparition that reveals possible future consequences of Scrooge's actions. | An apparition that shows potential future events, depicted as shadowy and ominous. | The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is a spectral entity that shows Scrooge visions of the future, including the death of Tiny Tim and the consequences of his actions.
nodes.id: Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come

nodes.name: Philadelphia
nodes.description: Philadelphia is one of the cities where J. B. Lippincott Company was based and published the 1915 edition of A Christmas Carol.

nodes.name: Ghost of Jacob Marley
nodes.description: The spectral form of Ebenezer Scrooge's former business partner, appearing as a warning.

nodes.name: Joe
nodes.description: A marine-store dealer and receiver of stolen goods. | Joe is a character who questions a woman about taking down bed-curtains, suggesting involvement in a post-mortem scene.

nodes.name: Ebenezer Scrooge
nodes.description: A grasping and covetous old man, the surviving partner of the firm Scrooge and Marley. | Ebenezer Scrooge is a character who experiences confusion about time, questions the reality of a ghostly visitation, and struggles with sleep and perception after encountering Marley's Ghost. | Ebenezer Scrooge is a character who experiences a transformative vision of his future death and vows to change his ways after being shown his own grave.

nodes.name: Mr. Topper
nodes.description: A bachelor, mentioned in the narrative context.

nodes.name: Dick Wilkins
nodes.description: A fellow apprentice of Ebenezer Scrooge. | Dick Wilkins was Scrooge's fellow apprentice and a close associate, remembered fondly by Scrooge.

nodes.name: Belle
nodes.description: An old sweetheart of Ebenezer Scrooge, later a comely matron. | Belle is the wife of a man who once knew Scrooge; she is portrayed as warm and affectionate, and her past relationship with Scrooge is referenced.

nodes.name: Caroline
nodes.description: The wife of one of Scrooge's debtors. | Caroline is a woman who anxiously awaits news from her husband regarding the death of a man who owed them money. | Caroline is the wife of a man who has died; she experiences relief at the news of his death, followed by guilt and prayer for forgiveness.

nodes.name: Mrs. Cratchit
nodes.description: The wife of Bob Cratchit and mother of Belinda and Martha. | Mrs. Cratchit is Bob Cratchit's wife, who dresses modestly but proudly in cheap ribbons to create a festive appearance for her family. | Mrs. Cratchit is the mother of the Cratchit family, who warmly greets her children and daughter Martha upon their return on Christmas Day. | Mrs. Cratchit is the mother of the Cratchit family who prepares the gravy, sweetens the apple sauce, and carves the goose. | Mrs. Cratchit is the homemaker who prepares the Christmas dinner, including the goose and pudding, and experiences nervous anticipation before serving the pudding. | Mrs. Cratchit expresses strong resentment toward Scrooge but toasts his health for the sake of Christmas Day and her family. | Mrs. Cratchit is Bob's wife, who expresses affection for her husband and believes in the goodness of Mr. Scrooge's nephew. | Mrs. Cratchit is Bob Cratchit's wife and mother to their children, who shows affection and emotional warmth toward her family.

nodes.name: Belinda Cratchit
nodes.description: Daughter of Bob and Mrs. Cratchit. | Belinda Cratchit is the second daughter of Bob and Mrs. Cratchit, who helps set the table and wears ribbons to appear festive.

nodes.name: Martha Cratchit
nodes.description: Daughter of Bob and Mrs. Cratchit. | Martha Cratchit is the daughter of the Cratchit family, who arrives late on Christmas Day after working the previous night and is affectionately welcomed by her mother and father. | Martha Cratchit is an apprentice at a milliner's who works long hours and plans to rest on Christmas Day, which she spends at home.

nodes.name: Mrs. Dilber
nodes.description: A laundress employed by or associated with Scrooge. | A woman who arrives at Old Joe's shop with a heavy bundle, identified as the laundress. She engages in a bold, confrontational exchange with the other women, defending her right to take care of herself. | Mrs. Dilber is a laundress who participates in the division of goods taken from a deceased man's belongings and engages in conversation about moral justification.

nodes.name: Fan
nodes.description: The sister of Ebenezer Scrooge.

nodes.name: Mrs. Fezziwig
nodes.description: The worthy partner of Mr. Fezziwig, known for hosting festive gatherings. | Mrs. Fezziwig is the wife of Fezziwig, described as having a vast, substantial smile and present at the Christmas celebration. | Mrs. Fezziwig is Fezziwig's dance partner, described as worthy of him in every sense, and equally skilled and spirited in dancing. | Mrs. Fezziwig is Fezziwig's wife, who participates in the Christmas ball and shares in the act of bidding farewell to guests with warm wishes.

nodes.name: Stave One--Marley's Ghost
nodes.description: The first chapter of the narrative, introducing the ghost of Jacob Marley.

nodes.name: Stave Two--The First of the Three Spirits
nodes.description: The second chapter of the narrative, focusing on the Ghost of Christmas Past.
nodes.id: Stave Two--The First of the Three Spirits

nodes.name: Stave Three--The Second of the Three Spirits
nodes.description: The third chapter of the narrative, focusing on the Ghost of Christmas Present.
nodes.id: Stave Three--The Second of the Three Spirits

nodes.name: Scrooge
nodes.description: Scrooge is a character who visits his nephew Fred and declares he will no longer tolerate certain behavior, indicating a change in attitude. | Scrooge is a businessman who was the sole partner, executor, administrator, and mourner of Marley; he continues to use the firm name 'Scrooge and Marley' after Marley's death. | Scrooge is a tight-fisted, cold, and solitary businessman who is known for his miserly nature and indifference to human sympathy. | Scrooge is a wealthy, miserly man who runs a counting-house and is hostile toward Christmas and generosity. | Scrooge is a bitter and cynical man who despises Christmas and expresses hostility toward those who celebrate it. | Scrooge is a miserly man who rejects festive cheer and refuses to engage with family or charitable requests, despite being the surviving partner of the deceased Mr. Marley. | Scrooge is a miserly individual who rejects charitable appeals during the Christmas season and believes the poor should rely on existing institutions like prisons and workhouses. | Scrooge is a character who resumes his labours with improved self-opinion and a more facetious temper, and is observed by a caroler at his door. | Scrooge is a stern, miserly employer who resents giving his clerk time off for Christmas and displays hostility toward holiday cheer. | Scrooge is the sole resident of a dark, old house in London, known for his lack of imagination and his previous relationship with Marley. | Scrooge is a cautious and solitary man who checks his rooms for intruders, values darkness, and is meticulous about securing his home. | Scrooge is a character who is cautious, solitary, and haunted by memories of Jacob Marley; he engages in ritualistic evening routines and experiences supernatural phenomena. | Scrooge is a skeptical and cold-hearted individual who encounters the ghost of his deceased partner, Jacob Marley, and struggles to accept the supernatural event. | Scrooge is a skeptical character who questions the reality of the Ghost, using humor to cope with fear and discomfort. | Scrooge is a character who encounters the ghost of Jacob Marley and is confronted with the consequences of his worldly life and neglected spiritual duties. | Scrooge is a character who engages in a dialogue with the ghost of Jacob Marley, reflecting on his past actions and the consequences of neglecting human compassion. | Scrooge is a miserly man who is visited by the ghost of his deceased partner, Jacob Marley, and is warned of future spiritual visitations. | Scrooge is the protagonist who witnesses spectral phenomena and experiences emotional turmoil after encountering ghosts and phantoms. | Scrooge is a character who listens to the striking of a clock and is confronted by an unearthly visitor in his bedroom. | Scrooge is a character who encounters the Ghost of Christmas Past and questions its identity and purpose. | Scrooge is a man who experiences a supernatural journey with a Spirit, recalling memories of his childhood and past. | Scrooge is a character who reflects on his lonely childhood and is emotionally moved by memories of his past, particularly during Christmas. | The main character who experiences vivid, nostalgic visions of fictional characters and stories during a supernatural encounter. | A young boy who was left at school during the holidays, later reunited with his sister and taken home by his father's changed kindness. | Scrooge is a character who recalls past events, including his apprenticeship and relationships, and is visited by a ghost. | Scrooge is a character who recalls his past as a young apprentice under Fezziwig, reflecting on his former self and relationships. | Scrooge is a former employee of Fezziwig who, during a supernatural experience, reflects on his past and is moved by the memory of Fezziwig's kindness. | A man consumed by greed and avarice, who is shown visions of his past by the Ghost, revealing his emotional transformation and loss of love. | Scrooge is a character who is confronted with memories of a past relationship and resists further visions from the Ghost. | Scrooge is a character who observes a family scene and reflects emotionally on childhood memories and desires. | Scrooge is a solitary figure who observes memories of others' joy and is distressed by visions of his own isolation and potential fate. | Scrooge is the protagonist who interacts with supernatural spirits, including extinguishing the light of a ghostly figure and experiencing a transition between dream and wakefulness. | Scrooge is a character who experiences fear and astonishment in response to unexplained phenomena, particularly a mysterious light and a transformed room. | Scrooge is a character who encounters the Ghost of Christmas Present and undergoes a transformation, showing humility and receptiveness to the Spirit's lessons. | Scrooge is a character who interacts with a robe and experiences a magical transformation on Christmas morning. | Scrooge is a character who observes and questions the Spirit's actions regarding Christmas dinner rituals and human goodwill. | Scrooge is a wealthy man who is challenged by the Spirit over his views on closing places of worship and denying workers their day of rest. | Scrooge is a miserly man who is confronted by the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come and is moved by the plight of Tiny Tim and the Cratchit family. | Scrooge is portrayed as an odious, stingy, and unfeeling man, referred to as the 'Ogre of the family' and the 'Founder of the Feast' sarcastically by Bob Cratchit. | Scrooge observes the family's humble yet joyful Christmas evening and is particularly attentive to Tiny Tim. | Scrooge is a character who accompanies the Ghost on a journey, questioning the places they visit and reacting with horror to the oceanic scene. | Scrooge is a character who observes supernatural scenes involving Christmas, including a lighthouse, a ship, and his nephew's celebration. | Scrooge is a wealthy but miserly old man who dismisses Christmas as a humbug and is criticized by his family for not using his wealth to bring joy. | Scrooge is a solitary individual who dislikes social gatherings, particularly at Christmas, and is described as having a gloomy disposition. | Scrooge is a character who is shaken by memories triggered by music and becomes emotionally softened, reflecting on his past and the kindnesses he neglected. | Scrooge is a character who participates in party games, shows unexpected engagement and insight, and is observed by a Ghost. | Scrooge is a character who is the subject of a guessing game, described as a disagreeable, savage animal-like figure living in London, not confined to a menagerie or market, and associated with merriment despite his gruff reputation. | Scrooge is a character who undergoes a transformative experience with a ghost during the Christmas holidays, observing societal suffering and moral lessons. | Scrooge is the protagonist who encounters the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come and is confronted with the figures of Ignorance and Want. | Scrooge is a character who encounters the Spirit of the Future and is deeply unsettled by its silent, ominous presence. | Scrooge is a wealthy businessman whose death is the subject of casual conversations among others, revealing his social isolation. | Scrooge is a character who reflects on mysterious conversations, anticipates future revelations about himself, and observes his surroundings with growing unease. | Scrooge is a character who witnesses a vision of a deceased man's lonely death and reflects on his own potential fate, showing fear and remorse. | Scrooge is a character who reflects on the death of a man and is deeply affected by the isolation and neglect surrounding it. | Scrooge is a character who witnesses the emotional aftermath of a man's death and is shown the Cratchit family by the Ghost. | Scrooge is the protagonist who undergoes a transformative journey through visions of the future, culminating in a confrontation with his own mortality. | Scrooge is a character who undergoes a profound emotional transformation after encounters with supernatural spirits, expressing joy, repentance, and renewed enthusiasm for life. | Scrooge is a character who experiences a transformative realization on Christmas Day, deciding to buy a large turkey for Bob Cratchit as an act of generosity. | Scrooge is a character who undergoes a transformation, purchasing a turkey, paying for a cab, and recompensing a boy with cheerful chuckles; he later greets others with warmth and kindness. | Scrooge is a character who undergoes a transformation, showing generosity and kindness by offering back-payments, visiting church, interacting with strangers, and unexpectedly visiting his nephew. | Scrooge is a businessman who initially appears stern and controlling but undergoes a transformation, deciding to raise Bob Cratchit's salary and support his family. | Scrooge is a character who undergoes a profound transformation, becoming generous, kind, and wise, and is known for keeping Christmas well after his change of heart.

nodes.name: The Woman
nodes.description: A woman who confirms to Joe that she took down bed-curtains, rings and all, from a deceased person's room. | A woman who laughs at the old man's death and mocks his life, celebrating the financial gain obtained from his possessions.

nodes.name: Tim
nodes.description: Tim is referenced as the person for whom Scrooge served as a blood-horse on the way from church.

nodes.name: Old Scratch
nodes.description: Old Scratch is a colloquial reference to the devil, invoked in dialogue to imply someone has met their demise. | Old Scratch is a colloquial reference to the devil, used to imply Scrooge's death and the belief that he has been claimed by evil.

nodes.name: Bed-Curtains
nodes.description: Bed-curtains are physical items removed from a bed, including rings, after someone's death, as discussed by Joe and the woman. | Items of household linen taken from the deceased man's room, identified and appraised by Old Joe. | Bed-curtains are household items stolen from the deceased man's room, removed along with their rings, and sold to Old Joe. | Bed-Curtains are fabric furnishings that Scrooge handles ecstatically, indicating his disoriented but euphoric state after his visions.

nodes.name: Decanter of Curiously Light Wine
nodes.description: A decanter containing unusually light wine, produced by a character, likely to accompany a meal. | A wine served in the best parlor to Scrooge and his sister, described as unusually light in weight.

nodes.name: Block of Curiously Heavy Cake
nodes.description: A cake of unusual weight, served alongside the light wine, indicating contrast in physical properties. | A cake served in the best parlor to Scrooge and his sister, described as unusually heavy in weight.

nodes.name: Stave One
nodes.description: The first section of a narrative work, marked by headings and tailpieces, introducing key characters and scenes. | The first section of the narrative, introducing Marley's death and Scrooge's character.

nodes.name: Stave Two
nodes.description: The second section of a narrative work, marked by headings and tailpieces, containing scenes involving Tim and other characters. | Stave Two is a section of the narrative detailing Scrooge's encounter with the first of the three spirits after midnight.

nodes.name: Stave Three
nodes.description: The third section of a narrative work, marked by headings and tailpieces, referencing climate and the character Tim.

nodes.name: Stave Four
nodes.description: The fourth section of a narrative work, marked by a heading, likely depicting a darker or more ominous phase of the story.

nodes.name: Stave Five
nodes.description: The fifth and final section of a narrative work, marked by a heading and tailpiece, suggesting resolution or conclusion. | The final section of the narrative, referenced by its heading numbers.

nodes.name: Tailpiece to Stave One
nodes.description: A decorative or illustrative element concluding Stave One of the narrative.

nodes.name: Tailpiece to Stave Two
nodes.description: A decorative or illustrative element concluding Stave Two of the narrative.

nodes.name: Tailpiece to Stave Five
nodes.description: A decorative or illustrative element concluding Stave Five of the narrative.

nodes.name: List of Coloured Illustrations
nodes.description: A list in the publication containing colored illustrations, marked by a tailpiece.

nodes.name: List of Black and White Illustrations
nodes.description: A list in the publication containing black and white illustrations, marked by a tailpiece.
nodes.id: List of Black and White Illustrations

nodes.name: IN BLACK AND WHITE
nodes.description: An illustration caption or heading indicating the style of a visual element in the publication.

nodes.name: Marley
nodes.description: Marley was Scrooge's business partner who is confirmed dead, with his name remaining above the warehouse door and his funeral attended by Scrooge. | Marley is a former business partner of Scrooge, associated with the firm Scrooge and Marley, and shares the same public identity in business. | Marley was Scrooge's deceased business partner, whose face appears on the door knocker, haunting Scrooge after seven years of death. | Marley is a deceased individual whose ghostly presence is anticipated by Scrooge, as suggested by his fear of Marley's pigtail appearing in the hall. | Marley is referenced as a previous occupant of Scrooge's room, implying a historical connection to the space and its supernatural context.

nodes.name: Scrooge and Marley
nodes.description: The business firm operated by Scrooge and Marley, which continues to bear Marley's name even after his death. | Scrooge and Marley is a business firm known for its association with Scrooge and his deceased partner Marley.

nodes.name: Marley's Ghost
nodes.description: The title of the opening illustration and section of the story, indicating the supernatural element introduced in Stave One. | Marley's Ghost is the spectral apparition of Jacob Marley, transparent and bound by a chain, appearing to warn Scrooge of the consequences of his life choices. | Marley's Ghost is a spectral figure bound by chains, representing the consequences of a life lived without compassion. | Marley's Ghost is a spectral entity that warned Ebenezer Scrooge of an impending visitation, causing him significant psychological distress and preoccupation.

nodes.name: Door-nail
nodes.description: A metaphorical object used to emphasize the certainty of Marley's death, symbolizing something unquestionably dead.

nodes.name: Coffin-nail
nodes.description: A metaphorical object considered by the narrator as potentially deadlier than a door-nail, though not used in the original simile.

nodes.name: Change
nodes.description: A reference to the financial district or business exchange where Scrooge's reputation for reliability was well established. | Change is a bustling commercial center in the city where merchants gather, conduct business, and discuss Scrooge's death.

nodes.name: Christmas Eve
nodes.description: Christmas Eve is the specific time when Scrooge is depicted as working in his counting-house, emphasizing the contrast between his demeanor and the festive season. | Christmas Eve is the day of celebration marked by the clerk's joyful activities and Scrooge's isolation. | Christmas Eve is the specific time when the ghost appears to Scrooge, marking a pivotal moment of spiritual reckoning and the passage of seven years since Marley's death. | Christmas Eve is the occasion on which Fezziwig halts work and hosts a joyful celebration for employees and guests.

nodes.name: Counting-House
nodes.description: The counting-house is Scrooge's workplace, where he conducts his business activities in cold, isolated conditions. | The counting-house is Scrooge's workplace, where he conducts business and interacts with his clerk. | The counting-house is Scrooge's workplace, where the portly gentleman had previously approached him for a charitable contribution.

nodes.name: Frosty Rime
nodes.description: Frosty rime is a physical manifestation of Scrooge's inner coldness, appearing on his head, eyebrows, and chin.

nodes.name: Flint
nodes.description: Flint is used metaphorically to describe Scrooge's hard and unyielding nature, from which no generosity or warmth can be extracted.

nodes.name: Blind Men's Dogs
nodes.description: The dogs of blind men recognize Scrooge's malevolent presence and react by avoiding him, symbolizing his feared and unwelcome nature.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Nephew
nodes.description: Scrooge's nephew is a cheerful, optimistic man who visits Scrooge to wish him a Merry Christmas and challenges his cynical views. | Scrooge's nephew is a cheerful and persistent individual who attempts to reconcile with Scrooge during the Christmas season and invites him to dinner. | Scrooge's nephew is a cheerful character who laughs heartily and embodies the spirit of Christmas, later appearing in a bright room with Scrooge and the Ghost. | Scrooge's nephew is a cheerful and humorous character who laughs heartily at Christmas and defends his uncle despite his miserly nature. | Scrooge's nephew is a good-natured, cheerful individual who invites Scrooge to dinner and pities him, believing Scrooge misses out on joy by isolating himself. | Scrooge's nephew is part of the festive family gathering and is implied to have colluded with Topper during the blind man's buff game. | Scrooge's nephew organizes the game 'Yes and No' and is delighted by his aunt's performance in party games.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Counting-House
nodes.description: Scrooge's counting-house is a cold, dimly lit workspace where Scrooge oversees his clerk and keeps control over resources like coal.

nodes.name: The Clerk
nodes.description: The clerk is Scrooge's underpaid assistant who works in a cold, cramped space and is denied adequate heating. | The clerk is Scrooge's subordinate who politely requests Christmas day off and responds with quiet joy to the holiday despite his circumstances.

nodes.name: Coal
nodes.description: Coal is the fuel used for heating, tightly controlled by Scrooge and withheld from the clerk.

nodes.name: Christmas
nodes.description: Christmas is a festive holiday symbolizing joy and generosity, which Scrooge rejects as 'humbug' while his nephew embraces it. | Christmas is portrayed as a time of spiritual significance, generosity, and emotional openness, contrasting with Scrooge's materialistic worldview. | Christmas is the festive season during which charitable efforts are made to aid the poor, and against which Scrooge's indifference is sharply contrasted. | Christmas represents the spirit of generosity, joy, and community celebrated by Fezziwig and his employees. | The seasonal context of the scene, associated with family, generosity, and emotional warmth. | Christmas is a festive spirit embodied by the Ghost, representing joy, generosity, and communal celebration, even in the harshest environments. | Christmas is a festive and reflective period associated with kindness, remembrance, music, and human connection, observed by the lighthouse keepers, ship crew, and Scrooge's nephew. | Christmas is a festive occasion symbolizing joy, generosity, and good humor, which Scrooge rejects but is celebrated by his nephew and niece. | Christmas is a festive occasion symbolizing merriment, family, and generosity, which Scrooge rejects but which others believe he should embrace. | The festive season during which the gathering occurs, symbolizing childhood innocence and the spirit of generosity. | Christmas represents a time of warmth, memory, and emotional reconciliation in the Cratchit household, symbolized by the decorated room above. | Christmas is the festive occasion referenced multiple times, marking the time of Scrooge's transformation and the greetings exchanged between characters. | A holiday celebration referenced as the occasion for Bob Cratchit's tardiness and Scrooge's subsequent change of heart.

nodes.name: Fog
nodes.description: Fog is a dense, oppressive atmospheric condition enveloping the city, contributing to the bleak and cold atmosphere.

nodes.name: Candles
nodes.description: Candles are artificial light sources burning in neighboring offices, providing dim illumination through the foggy air.

nodes.name: White Comforter
nodes.description: The white comforter is a garment worn by the clerk in an attempt to stay warm in the absence of adequate heating. | The clerk's white comforter is a simple, inadequate winter garment that contrasts with Scrooge's wealth and warmth.

nodes.name: Nephew
nodes.description: Scrooge's nephew is a cheerful and compassionate individual who defends Christmas as a time of goodwill and human connection. | The only child of the woman who died, and Scrooge's nephew, as confirmed by the Ghost and Scrooge.

nodes.name: Clerk
nodes.description: The clerk is Scrooge's employee who silently applauds the nephew's speech about Christmas but quickly suppresses his reaction out of fear.

nodes.name: Merry Christmas
nodes.description: A phrase used by others to express holiday cheer, which Scrooge rejects as hypocritical and financially burdensome. | A festive greeting exchanged by travelers, evoking nostalgia and emotional resonance in Scrooge. | Merry Christmas represents a festive, communal tradition that Scrooge initially dismisses but which evokes emotional memories of connection and joy. | Merry Christmas is a traditional seasonal greeting exchanged by Fezziwig and his guests, symbolizing goodwill and festive cheer. | Merry Christmas is a festive occasion celebrated by Fred and his guests, marked by toasting to Scrooge's health despite his aloofness.

nodes.name: Pudding
nodes.description: A traditional food item referenced by Scrooge in a violent metaphor, symbolizing his disdain for Christmas celebrants.

nodes.name: Holly
nodes.description: A plant used symbolically by Scrooge in a violent fantasy, representing the festive decoration he associates with Christmas. | A branch of fresh green holly held by the unearthly visitor, symbolizing winter despite the figure's summer adornments. | A plant with crisp leaves that reflect light, part of the festive greenery decorating the transformed room. | Holly is a plant with red berries and evergreen leaves, used decoratively in the Ghost's attire and the festive scene. | Holly is a decorative plant associated with Christmas, which vanishes during the magical transformation.

nodes.name: Parliament
nodes.description: A political institution referenced by Scrooge sarcastically, implying the nephew's eloquence could be suited for public office.

nodes.name: Mr. Marley
nodes.description: Mr. Marley was Scrooge's business partner who died seven years ago; his legacy of generosity is referenced by the charitable visitors.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Clerk
nodes.description: Scrooge's clerk is a low-paid employee with a family who warmly returns holiday greetings, contrasting Scrooge's coldness.

nodes.name: Portly Gentlemen
nodes.description: Two charitable gentlemen who visit Scrooge's office to solicit donations, representing a benevolent cause tied to the Christmas season.

nodes.name: The Gentleman
nodes.description: The gentleman is a charitable collector who approaches Scrooge to raise funds for the poor during Christmas, advocating for compassion and aid. | The gentleman is a recipient of Scrooge's unexpected generosity, reacting with astonishment and gratitude to Scrooge's offer of back-payments.

nodes.name: Prisons
nodes.description: Prisons are institutions referenced by Scrooge as the primary means of addressing poverty, which he believes are sufficient and should remain in operation.

nodes.name: Union Workhouses
nodes.description: Union workhouses are state-run facilities for the destitute, which Scrooge supports as adequate solutions to poverty, despite their harsh conditions.

nodes.name: Treadmill
nodes.description: The Treadmill is a punitive labor device used in workhouses, referenced by Scrooge as part of the system meant to deter idleness among the poor.

nodes.name: Poor Law
nodes.description: The Poor Law is a legal framework governing assistance to the poor, which Scrooge supports as fully effective and operational.

nodes.name: Surplus Population
nodes.description: Surplus population is a term used by Scrooge to dehumanize the poor, suggesting that their deaths would reduce societal burden. | Surplus population is a cold, utilitarian concept invoked by the Ghost to reflect Scrooge’s own earlier views on the worthlessness of the poor, particularly children like Tiny Tim.

nodes.name: Christian Cheer
nodes.description: Christian cheer refers to the moral and spiritual comfort that charitable aid aims to provide to the poor, particularly during Christmas.

nodes.name: Abundance
nodes.description: Abundance represents the wealth and prosperity that contrasts with the suffering of the poor, especially emphasized during the Christmas season.

nodes.name: The Lord Mayor
nodes.description: The Lord Mayor is a high-ranking official who orders his household to celebrate Christmas in a grand manner.

nodes.name: The Little Tailor
nodes.description: The little tailor, previously fined for drunkenness, prepares a Christmas pudding despite his poverty.

nodes.name: The Evil Spirit
nodes.description: The Evil Spirit is referenced metaphorically in relation to the biting cold, suggesting a supernatural or personified force.

nodes.name: St. Dunstan
nodes.description: St. Dunstan is a historical or legendary figure referenced in a metaphor comparing his confrontation with the Evil Spirit to the severity of the cold.

nodes.name: Brazier
nodes.description: A brazier is a fire container used by labourers to warm themselves in the cold street.

nodes.name: Water-Plug
nodes.description: A water-plug that overflows and freezes into ice due to the intense cold.

nodes.name: Ancient Tower of a Church
nodes.description: An ancient church tower with a Gothic window and a bell that strikes hours in the clouds, visible only intermittently in the fog.

nodes.name: Mansion House
nodes.description: The official residence and stronghold of the Lord Mayor, where Christmas preparations are ordered.

nodes.name: Christmas Carol
nodes.description: A traditional song sung by a caroler at Scrooge's door, beginning with 'God bless you, merry gentleman.' | A traditional Christmas song sung by a passerby outside Scrooge's office, which is met with hostility by Scrooge. | A song sung by a boy at Scrooge's door on Christmas Eve, which he regrets not having rewarded.

nodes.name: Holly Sprigs and Berries
nodes.description: Decorative plant elements that crackle in the heat of shop windows during Christmas.

nodes.name: Christmas Pudding
nodes.description: A traditional festive dish prepared by the little tailor for Christmas.

nodes.name: Beef
nodes.description: Food item purchased by the little tailor's wife for the Christmas meal.

nodes.name: Fog and Darkness
nodes.description: Atmospheric conditions that thicken and obscure visibility, contributing to the cold and eerie atmosphere.

nodes.name: Gas-Pipes
nodes.description: Urban infrastructure being repaired by labourers, with a fire lit nearby for warmth.

nodes.name: Cold
nodes.description: An intense, piercing environmental condition that permeates the scene, affecting people, objects, and mood.

nodes.name: Bargain and Sale
nodes.description: Economic principles contrasted with the festive, almost magical atmosphere of the Christmas shops.

nodes.name: Misanthropic Ice
nodes.description: Ice formed from the water-plug’s overflow, described with human-like hostility due to the extreme cold.

nodes.name: Flaring Links
nodes.description: Torches or lamps carried by people to guide horse-drawn carriages through the thick fog.

nodes.name: Gothic Window
nodes.description: A window in the church tower through which the bell appears to peer down at Scrooge.

nodes.name: Cornhill
nodes.description: Cornhill is a location in London where the clerk slides down a snowy slope in celebration of Christmas Eve.

nodes.name: Camden Town
nodes.description: Camden Town is the residential area to which the clerk rushes home after celebrating Christmas Eve. | Camden Town is the destination to which Scrooge intends to deliver the turkey.

nodes.name: Banker's Book
nodes.description: The banker's book is a financial record used by Scrooge to pass the time in the evening after dinner.

nodes.name: Blind Man's Buff
nodes.description: Blind man's buff is a traditional children's game that the clerk plays at home on Christmas Eve. | A Christmas party game played by the family, in which Topper deliberately targets the plump sister, suggesting prearranged behavior. | Blind Man's Buff is a party game in which the Blind Man tries to catch a player, and the Plump Sister is one of the participants.

nodes.name: Greatcoat
nodes.description: A heavy outer garment worn by Scrooge, symbolizing his cold demeanor and contrast to the clerk's lack of one.

nodes.name: Gloomy Suite of Rooms
nodes.description: The gloomy suite of rooms is Scrooge's residence, once belonging to his deceased partner, reflecting his isolation and melancholy.

nodes.name: Frost
nodes.description: Frost is a natural phenomenon that permeates Scrooge's environment, symbolizing emotional coldness and detachment. | Frost is a natural accumulation on the windowpane that obstructs Scrooge's view of the outside world, contributing to his uncertainty about the time and weather. | Frost is a natural phenomenon that freezes water on the moors, preventing it from spreading and contributing to the desolate landscape.

nodes.name: Ruler
nodes.description: A measuring tool used by Scrooge to drive away the caroler, demonstrating his aggression and intolerance.

nodes.name: Tavern
nodes.description: The tavern is where Scrooge eats his solitary dinner, reinforcing his social isolation.

nodes.name: The Door Knocker
nodes.description: The door knocker is a large, ordinary object that transforms into the ghostly face of Marley, serving as a supernatural omen.

nodes.name: The House
nodes.description: The house is an old, dreary residence in London, occupied only by Scrooge, with other rooms rented as offices and a dark yard. | The house is the setting where the dead man's body lies, and where Scrooge witnesses the desolation of his passing.

nodes.name: The City of London
nodes.description: The City of London is the urban setting where Scrooge resides and is referenced as a place with a reputation for imaginative people.

nodes.name: The Fog and Frost
nodes.description: Fog and frost are natural atmospheric conditions that cling to the house's gateway, creating a somber and eerie atmosphere.

nodes.name: The Genius of the Weather
nodes.description: The Genius of the Weather is a personified abstract concept representing the mournful, oppressive atmosphere surrounding the house.

nodes.name: The Door
nodes.description: The door is a physical entry point to Scrooge's residence, equipped with screws and nuts holding a knocker, and is locked securely by Scrooge.

nodes.name: The Candle
nodes.description: The candle is a light source carried and trimmed by Scrooge as he moves through his house, providing minimal illumination in dark surroundings.

nodes.name: The Staircase
nodes.description: The staircase is a wide and spacious flight of stairs in Scrooge's residence, described as capable of accommodating a hearse, and is ascended slowly by Scrooge.

nodes.name: The Sitting Room
nodes.description: The sitting room is one of Scrooge's interior rooms, checked for safety and found to be in order with a small fire and household items present.

nodes.name: The Bedroom
nodes.description: The bedroom is one of Scrooge's interior rooms, inspected and confirmed to be free of intruders.

nodes.name: The Lumber Room
nodes.description: The lumber room is a storage area in Scrooge's home, noted as being in its usual disordered state with an old fire-guard, shoes, fish baskets, and other items.

nodes.name: The Gruel
nodes.description: The gruel is a small portion of food prepared for Scrooge, who has a cold, and is kept warm on the hob.

nodes.name: The Dressing-Gown
nodes.description: The dressing-gown is a garment hanging suspiciously on the wall, which Scrooge inspects to ensure no one is concealed behind it.

nodes.name: The Poker
nodes.description: The poker is a tool found in the lumber room, likely used for tending the fire.

nodes.name: The Knocker
nodes.description: The knocker is a fixed fixture on the door, held in place by screws and nuts, and is the object Scrooge checks for signs of Marley's presence.

nodes.name: Gas Lamps
nodes.description: Gas lamps are external light sources outside the building, insufficient to illuminate the entryway well, contributing to the darkness Scrooge prefers.

nodes.name: Echoes
nodes.description: Echoes are acoustic phenomena produced by the slamming of the door, resonating through the house and each room or cellar independently.

nodes.name: Darkness
nodes.description: Darkness is a condition Scrooge prefers and finds inexpensive, influencing his behavior and perception of his surroundings.

nodes.name: The Locomotive Hearse
nodes.description: The locomotive hearse is a hallucinatory or imagined image perceived by Scrooge in the gloom, symbolizing his preoccupation with death and the supernatural.

nodes.name: Jacob Marley
nodes.description: Jacob Marley is Scrooge's deceased business partner, whose ghostly image haunts Scrooge's thoughts and is associated with the supernatural events in the room. | Jacob Marley is the ghost of Scrooge's former business partner, appearing transparent and bound by a chain made of cash-boxes, keys, padlocks, ledgers, deeds, and heavy purses. | Jacob Marley is the ghost of Scrooge's deceased business partner, bound by a chain forged in life through his neglect of human connection and moral responsibility. | Jacob Marley is the ghost of Scrooge's deceased business partner, bound in chains and tormented by remorse for having ignored the welfare of mankind during his life. | Jacob Marley is the deceased partner of Scrooge, whose ghost appears to warn him of the consequences of his life choices. | Jacob Marley is a deceased figure whose intervention is responsible for the dispatch of the second spirit to Scrooge. | Jacob Marley is Scrooge's deceased former partner, referenced as having been buried by a sexton, symbolizing Scrooge's past isolation and moral neglect. | Jacob Marley is Scrooge's deceased former partner, whose prediction about the spirits Scrooge recalls upon the Ghost's appearance. | Jacob Marley is a deceased former business partner of Ebenezer Scrooge, invoked by Scrooge in his moment of redemption as a symbol of the consequences of a life lived without kindness. | Jacob Marley is a deceased spirit whose ghostly presence previously haunted Scrooge, marking the beginning of his transformation.

nodes.name: The Bell
nodes.description: The Bell is a disused bell in Scrooge's room that begins to ring mysteriously, signaling the approach of supernatural forces. | The bell is a time-telling mechanism that chimes from six to twelve, leading Scrooge to believe he has slept through an entire day and night. | The bell is suspended in the mansion's structure, once used for signaling, now silent and emblematic of abandonment.

nodes.name: The Fireplace
nodes.description: The Fireplace is an old, low-burning hearth built by a Dutch merchant, adorned with illustrated Dutch tiles depicting biblical scenes. | The Fireplace is the location in Scrooge's room where Jacob Marley's ghost sits, serving as a focal point of the encounter.

nodes.name: Dutch Tiles
nodes.description: Dutch Tiles are ornamental floor tiles in the fireplace that depict biblical figures and stories, which distract Scrooge from his thoughts of Marley.

nodes.name: The Cellar Door
nodes.description: The Cellar Door is a door in the building that opens with a booming sound, leading to the source of clanking noises associated with ghostly chains.

nodes.name: Ghosts in Haunted Houses
nodes.description: Ghosts in Haunted Houses is a cultural concept describing spectral beings that drag chains, which Scrooge recalls as he hears the clanking noise.

nodes.name: Gruel
nodes.description: Gruel is a simple, warm food that Scrooge prepares for himself on a cold night, consumed near the fireplace.

nodes.name: Dressing-Gown
nodes.description: The Dressing-Gown is a garment worn by Scrooge after removing his cravat, part of his nighttime routine.

nodes.name: Slippers
nodes.description: Slippers are footwear worn by Scrooge as part of his evening ritual to prepare for rest.

nodes.name: Nightcap
nodes.description: The Nightcap is a head covering worn by Scrooge during his nighttime routine.

nodes.name: Cains and Abels
nodes.description: Cains and Abels are biblical figures depicted on the Dutch tiles, representing themes of fratricide and divine judgment.

nodes.name: Pharaoh's Daughters
nodes.description: Pharaoh's Daughters are biblical figures depicted on the Dutch tiles, associated with stories of rescue and divine intervention.

nodes.name: Queens of Sheba
nodes.description: Queens of Sheba are biblical figures depicted on the Dutch tiles, symbolizing wisdom and pilgrimage.

nodes.name: Angelic Messengers
nodes.description: Angelic Messengers are depicted descending on clouds like feather-beds on the Dutch tiles, representing divine communication.

nodes.name: Abrahams
nodes.description: Abrahams are biblical figures depicted on the Dutch tiles, symbolizing faith and covenant.

nodes.name: Belshazzars
nodes.description: Belshazzars are biblical figures depicted on the Dutch tiles, associated with divine retribution and the writing on the wall.

nodes.name: Apostles Putting Off to Sea in Butter-Boats
nodes.description: Apostles Putting Off to Sea in Butter-Boats are whimsical biblical depictions on the Dutch tiles, combining religious imagery with domestic objects.
nodes.id: Apostles Putting Off to Sea in Butter-Boats

nodes.name: The Chain
nodes.description: The Chain is a spectral object worn by Jacob Marley's ghost, composed of cash-boxes, keys, padlocks, ledgers, deeds, and heavy purses wrought in steel, symbolizing his earthly burdens.

nodes.name: The Kerchief
nodes.description: The Kerchief is a folded cloth bound around the ghost's head and chin, a detail previously unnoticed by Scrooge that adds to the ghost's tangible presence.

nodes.name: The Dying Flame
nodes.description: The Dying Flame is the fire in Scrooge's room that leaps up when Marley's Ghost enters, as if reacting to his presence.

nodes.name: The Ghost
nodes.description: The Ghost is a spectral apparition that appears to Scrooge, exhibits supernatural traits such as agitated hair and clothing, and communicates with a haunting presence. | The Ghost is the spectral form of Jacob Marley, appearing to Scrooge to warn him of the spiritual consequences of a life lived without compassion or community. | The Ghost is the spectral apparition of Jacob Marley, appearing to Scrooge to warn him of impending hauntings by three spirits. | The Ghost is a supernatural guide who shows Scrooge visions of his past, including his time at school and his isolation as a child. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge through memories, commenting on past events and people. | A supernatural entity that shows Scrooge visions of his past, present, and future, urging reflection and change. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that forces Scrooge to witness painful memories and refuses his request to stop the visions. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity showing Scrooge visions of past events, and it resists Scrooge's attempts to escape its revelations. | The Ghost is a spectral entity with a bright light, unaffected by Scrooge's attempts to suppress it, and associated with a powerful, persistent illumination. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that rebukes Scrooge, prompting him to reflect on his worth and the suffering of others. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity associated with Christmas, exulting in mirth and generously spreading joy, visible to some but not recognized by others like the lamplighter. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge through visions of Christmas scenes, including a lighthouse and a ship at sea. | The Ghost accompanies Scrooge, observes his behavior during the party, and expresses approval of his engagement. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge, reveals societal injustices, and embodies the spirit of Christmas, aging rapidly as its brief existence concludes. | The Ghost is a spectral entity that guides Scrooge and points to the dead man's head, representing a moral or spiritual force. | The Ghost is a supernatural entity that shows Scrooge scenes related to death and emotional responses, including the Cratchit family's grief. | The Ghost is a spectral entity that shows Ebenezer Scrooge visions of possible futures, particularly his own neglected grave, and responds to his pleas with subtle signs of compassion.

nodes.name: Chain
nodes.description: The Chain is a symbolic, supernatural artifact forged by Jacob Marley during his lifetime through his selfish and narrow focus on money and business. | The chain is a physical manifestation of Jacob Marley's remorse, forged by his own neglect of moral responsibilities during life.

nodes.name: Counting House
nodes.description: The Counting House is the place where Jacob Marley and Scrooge conducted their business, representing the confined, materialistic world Marley inhabited in life.

nodes.name: Spiritual Duty
nodes.description: Spiritual Duty refers to the moral obligation to engage with others and contribute to human welfare during one's life, which Marley neglected and now suffers for after death.

nodes.name: On the Wings of the Wind
nodes.description: An illustration title referenced in the text, symbolizing the ghost's endless, restless travel after death.
nodes.id: On the Wings of the Wind

nodes.name: Mankind
nodes.description: Mankind represents the collective humanity that Jacob Marley claims was his true business, contrasting with his former focus on profit.

nodes.name: Charity
nodes.description: Charity is one of the moral duties Jacob Marley identifies as central to his true business, alongside mercy, forbearance, and benevolence.

nodes.name: Mercy
nodes.description: Mercy is cited by Jacob Marley as an essential component of his neglected business, emphasizing compassion over financial gain.

nodes.name: Forbearance
nodes.description: Forbearance is mentioned by Jacob Marley as a moral principle he failed to practice, representing patience and restraint toward others.

nodes.name: Benevolence
nodes.description: Benevolence is identified by Jacob Marley as a core duty he ignored, referring to the active desire to do good for others.

nodes.name: The Common Welfare
nodes.description: The common welfare is described by Jacob Marley as his true business, contrasting with his narrow focus on commercial dealings.

nodes.name: The Blessed Star
nodes.description: The Blessed Star symbolizes moral guidance and divine compassion, which Jacob Marley regrets having ignored while alive.

nodes.name: Immortal Creatures
nodes.description: Immortal creatures refer to spirits or souls condemned to eternal labor, as mentioned by Jacob Marley in describing the afterlife's burdens.

nodes.name: Eternity
nodes.description: Eternity is the infinite duration into which the good potential of the earth must pass, according to Jacob Marley's lament.

nodes.name: Mortal Life
nodes.description: Mortal life is described by Jacob Marley as insufficient for fulfilling the vast potential for good that every individual possesses.

nodes.name: Opportunities Misused
nodes.description: Opportunities misused refer to the moral chances Jacob Marley failed to act upon during his lifetime, leading to eternal regret.

nodes.name: The Rolling Year
nodes.description: The rolling year represents the cyclical passage of time, during which Jacob Marley experiences heightened suffering.

nodes.name: Poor Homes
nodes.description: Poor homes are the dwellings of the disadvantaged that Jacob Marley regrets not visiting, symbolizing neglected human connections.

nodes.name: Three Spirits
nodes.description: The Three Spirits are supernatural entities destined to visit Scrooge on three consecutive nights to offer him a chance to change his fate.

nodes.name: The First of the Three Spirits
nodes.description: The First of the Three Spirits is a supernatural entity that appears to Scrooge after midnight, marking the beginning of his ghostly visions.
nodes.id: The First of the Three Spirits

nodes.name: Phantoms
nodes.description: Phantoms are restless, chained spirits wandering the night, lamenting their inability to aid the living, many of whom were known to Scrooge in life.

nodes.name: The White Waistcoat Ghost
nodes.description: An individual phantom in a white waistcoat, bound by a monstrous iron safe, who cries out in despair over his inability to help a woman and infant.

nodes.name: Chains
nodes.description: Chains are physical restraints worn by the phantoms, symbolizing their spiritual bondage and guilt from earthly actions. | Chains are broken or废弃 metal links collected in the shop, suggesting confinement, loss, or past labor. | Rusty chains found among the collection of discarded metal objects in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Iron Safe
nodes.description: An iron safe attached to the ankle of the white waistcoat ghost, representing burdens of greed or unfulfilled responsibility.

nodes.name: The Invisible World
nodes.description: The Invisible World refers to the realm of spirits and supernatural entities that Scrooge briefly perceives, challenging his previous skepticism.

nodes.name: Humbug!
nodes.description: Humbug! is Scrooge's former catchphrase, representing his cynical disbelief, which he is unable to utter after his supernatural experiences.

nodes.name: The Repeater
nodes.description: The repeater is a mechanical device on Scrooge's watch that he uses to check the time, which also indicates twelve, reinforcing his confusion about the passage of time.

nodes.name: The Clock
nodes.description: The clock is an inaccurate timepiece that strikes twelve at a time when Scrooge believes it should be early morning, prompting his suspicion of mechanical failure.

nodes.name: The First of Exchange
nodes.description: The First of Exchange is a financial document referenced by Scrooge, indicating a payment due, whose validity depends on the existence of measurable days.

nodes.name: The Unearthly Visitor
nodes.description: An otherworldly figure resembling a child but with the appearance of an aged man, characterized by white hair, a youthful face, long muscular limbs, and emitting a bright light from its head. It holds a branch of holly and wears summer flowers, contradicting its wintry setting.

nodes.name: Extinctisher
nodes.description: A large extinguisher used by the unearthly visitor as a cap, held under its arm, presumably to control the light emanating from its head.

nodes.name: Lustrous Belt
nodes.description: A shining belt worn around the waist of the unearthly visitor, which sparkles and glitters intermittently, contributing to the figure's fluctuating appearance.

nodes.name: Summer Flowers
nodes.description: Flowers of summer used to trim the attire of the unearthly visitor, creating a striking contrast with the wintry holly and season.

nodes.name: Jet of Light
nodes.description: A bright, clear beam of light emanating from the crown of the unearthly visitor's head, illuminating its form and surroundings.

nodes.name: Clock
nodes.description: A timekeeping device whose chimes Scrooge listens to and counts, marking the passage of time from quarter past to the hour. | A timepiece in Fezziwig's warehouse that shows the hour of seven.

nodes.name: The Spirit
nodes.description: The Spirit is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge through memories of his past, touching him gently and leading him to familiar locations. | A supernatural entity guiding Scrooge through visions of his past, who responds to his emotions and prompts further scenes. | The Spirit is a supernatural entity who sprinkles incense and water from a torch to enhance good humor and kindness among people on Christmas Day. | The Spirit is a supernatural entity that accompanies Scrooge, illuminating scenes of domestic warmth and joy with its torch. | The Spirit is the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come, a silent and ominous phantom that shows Scrooge visions of the future and identifies Ignorance and Want as societal dangers. | The Spirit is an unseen entity that communicates through subtle gestures and directs Scrooge's attention toward the future, implying moral significance in ordinary events.

nodes.name: The Open Country Road
nodes.description: A rural road where Scrooge and the Spirit arrive after passing through a wall, marked by snow-covered fields and the absence of the city.

nodes.name: The Market-Town
nodes.description: A small town with a bridge, church, and winding river, where Scrooge recognizes landmarks and encounters joyful travelers.

nodes.name: Winter Day
nodes.description: A clear, cold winter day with snow on the ground, marking the setting of Scrooge's memory journey.

nodes.name: Shaggy Ponies
nodes.description: Ponies trotting along the road, ridden by boys who are part of the lively scene from Scrooge's past.

nodes.name: Country Gigs and Carts
nodes.description: Rural vehicles driven by farmers, part of the festive winter scene observed by Scrooge.

nodes.name: Ali Baba
nodes.description: Ali Baba is a figure from folklore who appears suddenly outside the window, depicted in foreign garments and leading an ass laden with wood. | A character from folklore who appears in Scrooge's vision, depicted as a foreign figure with an axe and leading an ass laden with wood.

nodes.name: The Mansion
nodes.description: The mansion is a once-grand but now decaying school building with damp walls, broken windows, overgrown grounds, and a dreary interior.

nodes.name: The School
nodes.description: The school is a neglected institution where a young Scrooge was left alone during holidays, symbolizing his childhood isolation.

nodes.name: The Feeble Fire
nodes.description: The feeble fire is a small, inadequate source of warmth in the schoolroom where the young Scrooge reads alone.

nodes.name: The Ass
nodes.description: The ass is a domesticated animal laden with wood, led by Ali Baba, appearing as a vivid, dreamlike figure outside the school window.

nodes.name: The Axe
nodes.description: The axe is a tool carried by Ali Baba, stuck in his belt, symbolizing a foreign, perhaps mythical, intrusion into Scrooge's memory.

nodes.name: The Weather-Cock
nodes.description: The weather-cock is a small, ornamental device atop the mansion's cupola, indicating wind direction and marking the decayed grandeur of the building.

nodes.name: The Dull Yard
nodes.description: The dull yard is the desolate outdoor area behind the mansion, where half-thawed waterspouts drip and silence prevails.

nodes.name: The Hall
nodes.description: The hall is the central, dreary interior space of the mansion, filled with cold, vast, and poorly furnished rooms.

nodes.name: The Room
nodes.description: The room is a long, bare, melancholy space in the mansion where the young Scrooge reads alone, lined with plain desks and forms. | A modest but comfortable room where the family gathers, filled with noise and activity during the Christmas scene.

nodes.name: Valentine
nodes.description: A character referenced by Scrooge in his vision, associated with his wild brother Orson.

nodes.name: Orson
nodes.description: Valentine's wild brother, mentioned by Scrooge in his vision of nostalgic stories.

nodes.name: The Sultan's Groom
nodes.description: A figure turned upside down by the Genii in Scrooge's vision, criticized for marrying the Princess.

nodes.name: The Princess
nodes.description: A figure married to the Sultan's Groom in Scrooge's vision, mentioned in a critical tone.

nodes.name: The Parrot
nodes.description: A green-bodied, yellow-tailed parrot with a lettuce-like growth on its head, called 'Poor Robin Crusoe' by Scrooge.

nodes.name: Robin Crusoe
nodes.description: The name Scrooge gave to the parrot after it returned from sailing around an island.

nodes.name: Friday
nodes.description: A character running for his life to a little creek in Scrooge's vision, likely referencing Robinson Crusoe's companion.

nodes.name: Damascus Gate

nodes.description: A decanter containing unusually light wine, served to young people during a festive gathering.

nodes.name: Block of Heavy Cake

nodes.name: Warehouse

nodes.name: Welsh Wig

nodes.name: City Streets

nodes.name: Christmas-time

nodes.description: The pathway leading from the school to the road, along which the chaise drove away with the children.

nodes.name: Ebenezer
nodes.description: The younger version of Scrooge, who was apprenticed to Fezziwig and responds to his call.

nodes.name: The Three Miss Fezziwigs

nodes.name: The Six Young Followers

nodes.name: The Warehouse

nodes.name: Fiddler

nodes.name: Milkman

nodes.name: Fire

nodes.name: Sir Roger de Coverley

nodes.name: Porter

nodes.name: Cold Roast

nodes.name: Cold Boiled

nodes.name: Negus

nodes.name: Dance

nodes.name: Forfeits

nodes.name: Beer

nodes.name: The Two Apprentices

nodes.name: The Ghost of Christmas Past
nodes.description: The Ghost of Christmas Past is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge through memories of his past, prompting reflection on his life and choices.

nodes.name: Christmas Ball
nodes.description: The Christmas ball is a festive gathering hosted by Fezziwig, featuring traditional dances and warm social interactions among employees and their families.

nodes.name: Advance and Retire
nodes.description: Advance and retire is a dance step performed during the Christmas ball, part of the traditional social dancing of the time.

nodes.name: Cork-Screw

nodes.name: Thread-the-Needle
nodes.description: Thread-the-Needle is a dance maneuver performed during the Christmas ball, involving partners weaving between each other in a specific pattern.

nodes.name: Happiness Through Small Gestures
nodes.description: Happiness through small gestures is the idea that joy and fulfillment can be created through seemingly insignificant acts of kindness and personal presence, as demonstrated by Fezziwig.

nodes.name: The Fair Young Girl
nodes.description: Scrooge's former fiancée, who ends their engagement because he has replaced her with the pursuit of wealth.

nodes.name: Gain
nodes.description: The dominant passion that has taken root in Scrooge, replacing his earlier aspirations and personal relationships with an obsession for wealth.

nodes.name: Poverty
nodes.description: A state of economic hardship that Scrooge once accepted but now fears, and which he believes the world wrongly condemns when contrasted with the pursuit of wealth. | Poverty is a condition observed by the Spirit, portrayed not as destitution but as a state rich in hope and resilience.

nodes.name: The Pursuit of Wealth
nodes.description: The driving force in Scrooge's life that has led to the erosion of his personal relationships and moral values.

nodes.name: The Contract

nodes.name: The Girl
nodes.description: The girl is a former love of Scrooge who releases him from their bond, citing his transformation and materialistic values. | The Girl is one of two children emerging from the Ghost's robe, depicted as wretched, ragged, and deformed, symbolizing Want and the consequences of societal neglect. | The girl is Fred's servant who answers the door for Scrooge and assists him in locating Fred in the dining room.

nodes.name: The Beautiful Young Girl
nodes.description: A young girl who grows into a comely matron, mother to several children, and central to the joyful family scene.

nodes.name: The Comely Matron
nodes.description: The former young girl, now a mother, who laughs heartily and enjoys the chaotic play of her children.

nodes.name: The Daughter

nodes.name: The Father
nodes.description: The father who returns home, greeted enthusiastically by his family, and arrives with Christmas toys and presents.

nodes.name: The Porters
nodes.description: A man who accompanies the father and is overwhelmed by the children's exuberant reception upon arrival.

nodes.name: The Children
nodes.description: A group of children whose boisterous, individualistic behavior creates a tumultuous yet joyful atmosphere.

nodes.name: Christmas Toys and Presents
nodes.description: Items brought home by the father, symbolizing holiday generosity and the center of the children's excited rush.

nodes.name: Braided Hair
nodes.description: A personal feature of the daughter, held in deep sentimental value by Scrooge as a symbol of innocence and beauty.

nodes.name: The Precious Little Shoe
nodes.description: A small item belonging to the daughter, representing childhood purity and the object of Scrooge's protective longing.

nodes.name: The Flushed and Boisterous Group

nodes.name: Playful Chaos
nodes.description: The unrestrained, joyful, and disorderly behavior of the children, which is celebrated rather than reprimanded by the adults.

nodes.name: Nostalgia
nodes.description: Scrooge's deep emotional longing for the innocence, touch, and freedom of childhood, as observed in the family scene.

nodes.name: The Husband

nodes.name: The Partner

nodes.name: The Baby

nodes.name: The Doll's Frying Pan
nodes.description: A small toy object used by the baby in a playful but alarming incident during the family gathering.

nodes.name: The Wooden Platter

nodes.name: The Parlour

nodes.name: The Top of the House

nodes.name: Scrooge's Office
nodes.description: The location where Scrooge is seen alone, illuminated by a candle, reflecting his isolation and emotional desolation. | Scrooge's office is a workplace that he once occupied but later finds occupied by another, signifying his loss of identity and legacy.

nodes.name: The Fireside

nodes.name: Irrepressible Affection

nodes.name: Joy, Gratitude, and Ecstasy

nodes.name: Christmas Day
nodes.description: Christmas Day is a festive occasion marked by communal dining, church attendance, and the expression of goodwill among people. | Christmas Day is the festive occasion on which the Cratchit family gathers for a modest but joyful celebration centered around the goose and family reunion. | Christmas Day is the festive occasion on which the Cratchit family gathers for a meal and reflects on themes of compassion and gratitude. | Christmas Day is the occasion around which the Cratchit family gathers, marked by a strained toast to Scrooge and a subsequent return to merriment. | Christmas Day is the day on which Scrooge awakens transformed, realizing he has not missed the holiday and deciding to act with kindness.

nodes.name: Incense
nodes.description: Incense is a substance sprinkled by the Spirit from his torch to restore good humor and promote kindness among people.

nodes.name: Torch

nodes.name: Dinner-Carriers

nodes.name: Good Humour

nodes.name: Pavement Smoke

nodes.name: Spirit

nodes.name: Master Peter Cratchit

nodes.name: Christmas Present

nodes.name: Seventh Day

nodes.name: Fifteen Shillings

nodes.name: Ribbons

nodes.name: Monstrous Shirt-Collar
nodes.description: A large, outdated shirt collar belonging to Bob Cratchit, given to his son Peter as a symbolic gift for the holiday.

nodes.name: Bob's Monstrous Shirt-Collar
nodes.description: Bob's monstrous shirt-collar is a piece of clothing passed down to him as a family heirloom, worn with pride on Christmas Day despite its size and discomfort.

nodes.name: The Goose
nodes.description: The goose is the central Christmas meal prepared by the Cratchit family, whose aroma is detected by the children outside the baker's shop. | The goose is the central dish of the Cratchit family's Christmas meal, prepared with great care and excitement. | The goose is the central dish of the Christmas meal, admired for its tenderness, flavor, size, and cheapness.

nodes.name: Sage and Onion
nodes.description: Sage and onion are aromatic ingredients used in the preparation of the Christmas goose, evoking anticipation and delight among the Cratchit children. | Sage and onion are used as stuffing for the goose, so abundant that the youngest Cratchits are described as being 'steeped' in them.

nodes.name: The Iron Frame
nodes.description: The iron frame is a supportive device used to assist Tiny Tim in standing and walking, indicating his physical disability.

nodes.name: The Comforter

nodes.name: Martha
