nodes.description: The pudding is cooked in a copper pot and is described as 'singing,' indicating it is a cherished part of the Christmas tradition. | The pudding is a Christmas dessert prepared by Mrs. Cratchit, described as hard, firm, and blazing with ignited brandy, decorated with holly. | The pudding is a central dish of the Cratchit Christmas dinner, symbolizing family tradition and the social expectation of abundance despite limited resources.

nodes.name: The Hot Mixture
nodes.description: The hot mixture is a drink made by Bob Cratchit from gin and lemons, simmered on the hob as part of the Christmas celebration.

nodes.name: The Carving-Knife
nodes.description: The carving-knife is used by Mrs. Cratchit to serve the goose, and its use is preceded by a moment of suspense. | The carving-knife is used by Mrs. Cratchit to cut into the goose, marking the moment of anticipation before serving.

nodes.name: The Crutch
nodes.description: Tiny Tim's crutch is a physical aid that he uses, and its sound on the floor signals his return to the family gathering.

nodes.name: The Church
nodes.description: The church is referenced by Tiny Tim as a place where he hopes people will see him on Christmas Day to remember divine miracles.

nodes.name: The Wash-House
nodes.description: The wash-house is where Tiny Tim is taken so he cannot hear the pudding cooking until it is time to return.

nodes.name: The Table
nodes.description: The table is where the Cratchit family gathers for their Christmas meal, with Tiny Tim seated beside Bob.

nodes.name: The Fire
nodes.description: The fire is the heating source beside which Tiny Tim is seated after returning from the wash-house. | The fire is the central point around which the family gathers, symbolizing warmth, togetherness, and comfort during their evening conversation.

nodes.name: The Copper
nodes.description: The copper is the large pot in which the pudding is boiled, producing a singing sound as it cooks. | The copper is the cooking vessel from which the pudding is removed, emitting steam and aromatic smells.

nodes.name: The Saucepan
nodes.description: The saucepan is used by Mrs. Cratchit to prepare the gravy beforehand.

nodes.name: The Apple Sauce
nodes.description: The apple sauce is sweetened by Miss Belinda as part of the Christmas meal preparation.

nodes.name: The Gravy
nodes.description: The gravy is made by Mrs. Cratchit and served hot with the goose.

nodes.name: The Pots and Pans
nodes.description: Collective kitchen utensils used in preparing the Christmas meal, including the jug, saucepan, and copper.

nodes.name: Apple Sauce
nodes.description: Apple sauce is served alongside the goose to supplement the meal.

nodes.name: Mashed Potatoes
nodes.description: Mashed potatoes are served as a side dish to extend the goose meal for the family.

nodes.name: Christmas Holly
nodes.description: Christmas holly is used decoratively, stuck into the top of the pudding to enhance its festive appearance.

nodes.name: Brandy
nodes.description: Brandy is ignited and used to flame the pudding, creating a dramatic visual and aromatic effect during serving.

nodes.name: The Cloth
nodes.description: The cloth is the wrapping used to steam the pudding, contributing to its distinctive smell upon removal.

nodes.name: The Cratchit Family
nodes.description: The Cratchit family is a poor but loving household that gathers around the hearth to celebrate Christmas with simple food and drink, embodying warmth and resilience.

nodes.name: Christmas Dinner
nodes.description: The Christmas dinner is a modest family meal prepared by the Cratchits, featuring a pudding, apples, oranges, and chestnuts, and followed by a heartfelt toast.

nodes.name: Christmas Toast
nodes.description: The Christmas toast is a ritual in which Bob Cratchit and his family express goodwill and blessings, culminating in Tiny Tim's words, 'God bless us every one!'

nodes.name: The Jug
nodes.description: The jug contains a hot beverage served to the Cratchit family, tasted and deemed perfect, and poured into makeshift glassware during the Christmas celebration.

nodes.name: The Glassware
nodes.description: The glassware consists of two tumblers and a custard cup without a handle, used by the Cratchit family to serve drinks, symbolizing their humble means.

nodes.name: Chestnuts
nodes.description: Chestnuts are roasted on the fire during the Cratchit Christmas celebration, adding auditory and aromatic warmth to the gathering. | Roasted nuts served during the family's Christmas evening, symbolizing simple domestic comfort.

nodes.name: Penitence and Grief
nodes.description: Penitence and grief are the emotional responses Scrooge experiences upon hearing his own harsh words echoed by the Ghost, marking a turning point in his character.

nodes.name: Five-and-sixpence
nodes.description: Five-and-sixpence is the weekly income projected for Peter Cratchit's potential new job, representing a significant financial improvement for the family.

nodes.name: Milliner's
nodes.description: The milliner's is the workplace where Martha Cratchit serves as an apprentice, subject to long working hours.

nodes.name: Countess
nodes.description: A noblewoman observed by Martha Cratchit, mentioned briefly as part of her experience outside the home. | A noblewoman seen by the family some days before, mentioned in conversation as a point of interest.

nodes.name: Lord
nodes.description: A nobleman observed by Martha Cratchit, mentioned alongside the countess in her account of social encounters. | A nobleman seen by the family, noted for being approximately the same height as Peter.

nodes.name: Peter
nodes.description: Peter is a member of a humble family who reacts to a comment about a lord's height by pulling up his collar, and may be familiar with pawnbrokers. | Peter is one of the Cratchit children who reads aloud from a book, likely the Bible, to the quiet family. | Peter is one of the Cratchit children who observes his father's changed walking pace and recalls memories of Tiny Tim. | Peter is one of Bob and Mrs. Cratchit's children, who is teased about possibly finding a romantic partner and securing a better job.

nodes.name: A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow
nodes.description: A song performed by Tiny Tim, depicting a child lost in snowy conditions, sung with a plaintive voice.
nodes.id: A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow

nodes.name: Christmas Holiday
nodes.description: A day off from work observed by the family at home, marked by togetherness, song, and simple pleasures.

nodes.name: Pawnbroker's
nodes.description: A place associated with financial hardship, possibly familiar to Peter, indicating the family's economic struggles.

nodes.name: Jug
nodes.description: A vessel used to serve drinks, passed around during the family's gathering along with chestnuts.

nodes.name: Roaring Fires
nodes.description: Fires in kitchens and parlours that provide warmth and light, emblematic of domestic celebration and hospitality.

nodes.name: Deep Red Curtains
nodes.description: Curtains ready to be drawn to exclude cold and darkness, indicating preparation for a cozy evening.

nodes.name: Hot Plates
nodes.description: Plates heated before the fire in preparation for a dinner, signifying domestic care and anticipation.

nodes.name: Hooded and Fur-Booted Girls
nodes.description: A group of elegantly dressed young women who walk together to a neighbor's house, described playfully as 'artful witches.'

nodes.name: Ghost
nodes.description: A benevolent supernatural being that exults in the joy of human connection and generosity during Christmas.

nodes.name: The Lamplighter
nodes.description: The Lamplighter is a minor character who runs ahead, lighting street lamps and laughing as the Ghost passes, unaware of the Ghost's presence.

nodes.name: The Miners
nodes.description: The Miners are a group of people living and working in the bowels of the earth, who celebrate Christmas with song and family despite their harsh environment.

nodes.name: The Old Man
nodes.description: The Old Man is a miner who sings a traditional Christmas song to his family, gaining energy from their participation in the chorus. | The old man is a deceased individual whose corpse is plundered by others after death; he was disliked in life and is remembered with contempt.

nodes.name: The Moors
nodes.description: The Moors are a bleak, desolate landscape with rudimentary stone formations, frozen water, and sparse vegetation, serving as a setting for the miners' dwelling.

nodes.name: The Sea
nodes.description: The Sea is a vast, violent body of water that Scrooge witnesses after leaving the moors, characterized by thundering waves and destructive power.

nodes.name: Christmas Song
nodes.description: A traditional Christmas song sung by the old miner and his family, passed down through generations and central to their festive gathering.

nodes.name: Moss
nodes.description: Moss is a plant that grows on the barren moors, one of the few forms of vegetation surviving in the harsh environment.

nodes.name: Furze
nodes.description: Furze is a coarse, spiny shrub that grows on the moors, alongside rank grass, as one of the few forms of plant life in the desolate region.

nodes.name: Coarse Rank Grass
nodes.description: Coarse rank grass is a type of vegetation found on the moors, growing in the harsh, frozen environment alongside moss and furze.

nodes.name: Hut
nodes.description: The Hut is a simple dwelling made of mud and stone, where the miners and their families gather to celebrate Christmas by the fire.

nodes.name: Lamplight
nodes.description: Lamplight is the artificial light produced by the lamplighter, dotting the dusky street with small points of illumination as he performs his evening duties.

nodes.name: The Lighthouse
nodes.description: A solitary lighthouse built on a reef of sunken rocks, where two men keep a fire and celebrate Christmas with grog and song.

nodes.name: The Ship
nodes.description: A ship at sea where all crew members, despite their circumstances, share Christmas thoughts, tunes, and kindness toward one another.

nodes.name: Grog
nodes.description: Grog is a drink consumed by the two men at the lighthouse during their Christmas celebration.

nodes.name: Seaweed
nodes.description: Seaweed clings to the base of the lighthouse and is described as being born of the water, contrasting with storm-birds born of the wind.

nodes.name: Storm-Birds
nodes.description: Storm-birds are avian creatures that rise and fall around the lighthouse, likened to the waves they skim.

nodes.name: The Wind
nodes.description: The wind is a natural force that creates storm-birds and moans over the sea, contributing to the atmosphere of the scenes observed by Scrooge.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Niece
nodes.description: Scrooge's niece is a pretty, earnest, and expressive woman who strongly disapproves of Scrooge's attitude toward Christmas and shares her husband's sentiments. | Scrooge's niece is an enthusiastic participant in the gathering, encouraging her brother Fred to continue speaking and expressing agreement with his views on Scrooge. | Scrooge's niece plays the harp and performs a simple tune that evokes memories of Scrooge's childhood, prompting emotional reflection. | Scrooge's niece is seated comfortably during the party, participates in forfeits, and excels at the game 'How, When, and Where.'

nodes.name: Laughter
nodes.description: Laughter is portrayed as an irresistible and contagious force of good humor that unites Scrooge's nephew, niece, and their friends in celebration.

nodes.name: Wealth
nodes.description: Wealth refers to Scrooge's financial resources, which are criticized for being unused and unshared, depriving others of potential benefit.

nodes.name: Topper
nodes.description: Topper is a bachelor who is visibly attracted to one of Scrooge's niece's sisters and responds dismissively when asked for his opinion on bachelors. | Topper is a member of the musical family who sings bass well and participates in blind man's buff, showing deliberate and humorous behavior toward the plump sister. | Topper is a guest at the party who knows that Scrooge's sisters are sharp players.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Niece's Sister (Plump, with Lace Tucker)
nodes.description: One of Scrooge's niece's sisters, described as plump with a lace tucker, who blushes when Topper expresses his views on bachelors.
nodes.id: Scrooge's Niece's Sister (Plump, with Lace Tucker)

nodes.name: Scrooge's Niece's Sister (With Roses)
nodes.description: One of Scrooge's niece's sisters, distinguished from the plump sister by having roses, and not the focus of Topper's attention.

nodes.name: Dinner
nodes.description: Dinner is a social gathering at which Scrooge's family is assembled, serving as a context for expressing opinions about Scrooge's isolation.

nodes.name: Mouldy Old Office
nodes.description: The mouldy old office is Scrooge's workplace, described as a lonely and uninviting place where he spends his time.

nodes.name: Dusty Chambers
nodes.description: The dusty chambers are Scrooge's private living quarters, portrayed as isolated and cheerless, contrasting with the warmth of the family gathering.

nodes.name: The Plump Sister
nodes.description: The plump sister is a participant in the blind man's buff game, targeted specifically by Topper, and protests that the game is unfair.

nodes.name: The Simple Little Air
nodes.description: A simple musical tune played on the harp by Scrooge's niece, which triggers memories of Scrooge's childhood and his time at the boarding school.

nodes.name: Glee
nodes.description: A type of musical piece performed by the family, showcasing their musical talent and festive spirit.

nodes.name: Catch
nodes.description: A type of round or canon song performed by the musical family during their evening entertainment.

nodes.name: Harp
nodes.description: A musical instrument played by Scrooge's niece, used to perform the tune that evokes Scrooge's memories.

nodes.name: Boarding-School
nodes.description: The place where Scrooge was sent as a child, referenced by the Ghost of Christmas Past and recalled when the simple tune is played.

nodes.name: Blind Man
nodes.description: The Blind Man participates in a game of blind man's buff and has a confidential interaction with a woman behind the curtains.

nodes.name: Plump Sister
nodes.description: The Plump Sister is pursued by the Blind Man during the game of blind man's buff and protests that the game is unfair. | The plump sister is a participant in the guessing game who correctly identifies Scrooge as the answer and joins in the celebration.

nodes.name: Yes and No
nodes.description: Yes and No is a party game where one person thinks of an object and others ask yes-or-no questions to guess it.

nodes.name: How, When, and Where
nodes.description: How, When, and Where is a party game in which Scrooge's niece excels, outperforming her sisters.

nodes.name: Live Animal
nodes.description: The live animal is the subject of the 'Yes and No' game, described as disagreeable, savage, and one that growls and grunts.

nodes.name: London
nodes.description: London is the city where Scrooge lives and moves about, not confined to public displays or institutions.

nodes.name: Happy New Year
nodes.description: Happy New Year is a celebratory occasion mentioned alongside Christmas, invoked in the toast to Scrooge.

nodes.name: Mulled Wine
nodes.description: Mulled wine is a warm, spiced drink served during the celebration, used in the toast to Scrooge's health.

nodes.name: Almshouse
nodes.description: An almshouse is a charitable residence for the poor, visited by the Spirit, where human suffering is met with compassion.

nodes.name: Hospital
nodes.description: A hospital is a place of healing visited by the Spirit, where the sick are depicted as cheerful despite their condition.

nodes.name: Gaol
nodes.description: A gaol (jail) is a place of confinement visited by the Spirit, symbolizing areas where society's marginalized are found.

nodes.name: Vain Man's Little Brief Authority
nodes.description: Vain man's little brief authority refers to human governance or control that fails to exclude the Spirit from places of suffering.

nodes.name: Guessing Game
nodes.description: The guessing game is a social activity in which participants deduce Scrooge’s identity through elimination, culminating in a joyful revelation.

nodes.name: Toasting
nodes.description: Toasting is a ritual act of raising a drink to honor someone, performed by Fred and his guests in honor of Scrooge.

nodes.name: The Boy
nodes.description: The Boy is one of two children emerging from the Ghost's robe, depicted as wretched, ragged, and deformed, symbolizing Ignorance and the consequences of societal neglect.

nodes.name: Twelfth-Night Party
nodes.description: A Christmas-related celebration that Scrooge and the Ghost attend, marking a moment when Scrooge notices the Ghost's physical aging.

nodes.name: Ignorance
nodes.description: Ignorance is symbolized by the boy emerging from the Ghost's robe, representing societal neglect and the lack of education or awareness among the poor. | Ignorance is personified as a boy, representing societal neglect and lack of education, and is identified by the Spirit as the greater threat among the two children.

nodes.name: Want
nodes.description: Want is symbolized by the girl emerging from the Ghost's robe, representing poverty, deprivation, and the material suffering of the underprivileged. | Want is personified as a girl, representing poverty and deprivation, and is presented alongside Ignorance as a consequence of societal failure.

nodes.name: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
nodes.description: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is a spectral entity that appears as a shrouded, silent phantom, showing Scrooge visions of future events. | The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is a silent, ominous specter that shows Scrooge visions of the future, particularly his own death and isolation.
nodes.id: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come

nodes.name: Spirit of the Future
nodes.description: The Spirit of the Future is a spectral entity that appears as a silent, shrouded figure with a pointing hand, intended to show Scrooge visions of his future.

nodes.name: Great Fat Man
nodes.description: A merchant at Change who is indifferent to Scrooge's death and only knows that he died last night.

nodes.name: Red-Faced Gentleman
nodes.description: A merchant at Change with a distinctive nose, who inquires about what Scrooge did with his money after his death.

nodes.name: Black Gloves
nodes.description: Black gloves are symbolic attire associated with mourning, mentioned by a speaker who claims disinterest due to never wearing them.

nodes.name: Lunch
nodes.description: Lunch is referenced as a condition for attending Scrooge's funeral, revealing the transactional and indifferent attitude toward his death.

nodes.name: Business Point of View
nodes.description: Business point of view refers to the narrow, professional perspective by which Scrooge valued his relationships, devoid of personal warmth.

nodes.name: Jacob
nodes.description: Jacob was Scrooge's old partner, whose death is referenced as a past event unrelated to the Spirit's current focus on the future.

nodes.name: The Phantom
nodes.description: The Phantom is a silent, ominous presence beside Scrooge, characterized by its outstretched hand and unseen eyes that observe him intently. | A spectral figure guiding Scrooge through visions of the future, pointing silently to the dead man's body as a warning.

nodes.name: The Porch
nodes.description: The Porch is an entrance point to a bustling area where Scrooge expects to see his own image but finds none, symbolizing his anticipated transformation.

nodes.name: The Obscure Part of Town
nodes.description: An impoverished, crime-ridden district with foul alleys, wretched buildings, and desperate inhabitants, where Scrooge has never been before but recognizes its reputation.

nodes.name: The Low-Browed Shop
nodes.description: A dilapidated shop in the impoverished district where discarded iron, rags, bones, and other refuse are bought and stored.

nodes.name: Rusty Keys
nodes.description: Rusty keys are among the discarded metal objects collected in the low-browed shop, symbolizing forgotten or abandoned access and ownership. | Discarded metal keys found in piles within Old Joe's shop, part of the assortment of refuse he deals in.

nodes.name: Nails
nodes.description: Nails are discarded metal fasteners found among refuse in the low-browed shop, indicative of decay and discarded utility. | Discarded nails present among the refuse items in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Hinges
nodes.description: Hinges are broken mechanical components found among refuse, symbolizing decayed connections or structures. | Broken or discarded hinges, part of the refuse iron collection in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Files
nodes.description: Files are worn metal tools found among refuse, indicating discarded utility and labor. | Metal files, worn and rusty, among the items sold by Old Joe.

nodes.name: Scales
nodes.description: Scales are weighing instruments found among refuse, possibly symbolizing lost fairness or measurement. | Old weighing scales, likely used for measuring scrap metal, present in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Weights
nodes.description: Weights are metal objects used for measurement, found among refuse, suggesting abandoned balance or order. | Metal weights, part of the refuse iron and discarded tools in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Refuse Iron
nodes.description: Refuse iron is scrap metal collected in the shop, representing discarded value and economic decay. | General term for discarded iron objects of various kinds collected and sold by Old Joe.

nodes.name: Future
nodes.description: The Future is the domain of the Spirit, contrasted with the past death of Jacob, and is the focus of Scrooge's introspection and anticipated moral lessons.

nodes.name: Moral Improvement
nodes.description: Moral improvement is the intended outcome Scrooge believes will arise from observing the Spirit's signs and his future self's conduct.

nodes.name: Crime
nodes.description: Crime is a pervasive condition in the obscure part of town, associated with filth, misery, and degradation.

nodes.name: Filth
nodes.description: Filth describes the physical and moral decay evident in the impoverished district, manifesting in dirt, smell, and squalor.

nodes.name: Misery
nodes.description: Misery characterizes the suffering of the people in the impoverished district, reflected in their appearance and living conditions.

nodes.name: Old Joe
nodes.description: An elderly man, nearly seventy years old, who deals in discarded items and operates a shop filled with refuse. He is described as a grey-haired rascal who smokes a pipe and has a casual, familiar relationship with his customers. | Old Joe is a receiver of stolen goods who appraises and pays for items taken from the deceased man's residence. | Old Joe is a character who buys stolen items from women after a man's death, and is described as being overly generous yet pragmatic about his dealings.

nodes.name: The Charwoman
nodes.description: A woman who entered Old Joe's shop first with a heavy bundle, identified by Mrs. Dilber as the charwoman. She is the first to arrive and initiates the group's recognition.

nodes.name: The Undertaker's Man
nodes.description: A man in faded black who arrives at Old Joe's shop shortly after the two women. He is startled by the others' presence and is identified by Mrs. Dilber as the undertaker's man.

nodes.name: Old Joe's Shop
nodes.description: A cluttered, dimly lit space filled with heaps of rusty keys, nails, chains, refuse iron, rags, corrupted fat, and bones. It includes a parlour area screened off by tattered curtains and a charcoal stove made of old bricks.

nodes.name: Unseemly Rags
nodes.description: Tattered, dirty fabrics piled in Old Joe's shop, used to screen off the parlour area and mixed with other refuse.

nodes.name: Corrupted Fat
nodes.description: Decomposed or spoiled organic fat matter found among the refuse in Old Joe's shop, suggesting long-term accumulation of waste.

nodes.name: Bones
nodes.description: Human or animal bones, described as sepulchres, found among the refuse in Old Joe's shop.

nodes.name: Charcoal Stove
nodes.description: A stove made of old bricks, used by Old Joe for warmth in his shop.

nodes.name: Frouzy Curtaining
nodes.description: A makeshift curtain made of miscellaneous tatters hung on a line to screen off the parlour area from the shop's cold air.

nodes.name: Smoky Lamp
nodes.description: An oil lamp in Old Joe's shop that is dim and smoky, requiring trimming with the stem of his pipe.

nodes.name: Stair-Rod
nodes.description: An old stair-rod repurposed by Old Joe to rake together the fire in his stove.

nodes.name: The Deceased Man
nodes.description: A deceased man, described as a 'wicked old screw,' whose possessions are being divided and sold by others after his death. | The deceased man is the owner of the stolen items, including bed-curtains and a shirt, whose body was left unattended and robbed after death. | The deceased man is the husband of Caroline and the creditor whose death brings relief to his debtors, though his identity is not explicitly named.

nodes.name: The Bundle
nodes.description: A bundle containing items taken from the deceased man's residence, including a seal, pencil-case, sleeve-buttons, and a brooch.

nodes.name: Sheets and Towels
nodes.description: Household textiles taken from the deceased man's residence and later appraised by Old Joe.

nodes.name: Silver Teaspoons
nodes.description: Two old-fashioned silver teaspoons taken from the deceased man's belongings and appraised by Old Joe.

nodes.name: Sugar-Tongs
nodes.description: A household utensil taken from the deceased man's residence and appraised by Old Joe. | Sugar-tongs are small household utensils among the stolen items sold to Old Joe.

nodes.name: Boots
nodes.description: Footwear taken from the deceased man's possessions and appraised by Old Joe. | Boots are personal footwear items taken from the deceased man and sold to Old Joe.

nodes.name: Moral Justification
nodes.description: The belief held by the characters that taking the deceased man's belongings is not sinful because he neglected others in life.

nodes.name: Appraisal
nodes.description: The process by which Old Joe evaluates the value of stolen items and records prices on the wall.

nodes.name: Chalked Prices
nodes.description: The recorded monetary values written on the wall by Old Joe for each item taken from the deceased man's residence.

nodes.name: The First Woman
nodes.description: The first woman is one of the individuals who stole belongings from a deceased man, including bed-curtains and a shirt, and justifies her actions with cold pragmatism.

nodes.name: The Shirt
nodes.description: The shirt is the best clothing belonging to the deceased man, which was stolen by the woman to prevent it from being buried with him.

nodes.name: Oil
nodes.description: Oil is a substance that the woman warns Old Joe not to spill on the blankets, indicating its potential to damage the stolen items.

nodes.name: Blankets
nodes.description: Blankets are items stolen from the deceased man's bed, which the woman is concerned about being soiled by oil.

nodes.name: Calico
nodes.description: Calico is a type of fabric mentioned by the woman as sufficient for burying the deceased man, implying it is a low-cost, common material.

nodes.name: The Bed
nodes.description: A bare, uncurtained bed on which the deceased man's body lies, uncovered and unattended, symbolizing neglect and isolation.

nodes.name: The Flannel Bag
nodes.description: A flannel bag containing money taken from the deceased man's possessions, used by others to distribute their gains.

nodes.name: Death
nodes.description: Death is personified as a force of dominion and dread, representing the finality and moral weight of the old man's lonely end.

nodes.name: Avarice
nodes.description: Avarice is the dominant trait associated with the old man and potentially Scrooge, representing greed and the corrosive effect of selfishness. | Avarice is the dominant trait associated with the dead man, representing greed and selfishness that led to his lonely end.

nodes.name: The Ragged Sheet
nodes.description: A ragged sheet covering the body of the deceased man, symbolizing neglect and the absence of care or dignity in death.

nodes.name: The Dead Man
nodes.description: The dead man is a figure who lived a life of avarice and isolation, with no one to mourn him or recall his kindness.

nodes.name: Caroline's Husband
nodes.description: Caroline's husband is a young, careworn man who delivers the news that the dead man has died and will not relent on his debts.

nodes.name: The Room of Death
nodes.description: The Room of Death is an empty, dark house where the dead man lies unattended, surrounded by restless animals.

nodes.name: The Kitchen
nodes.description: The kitchen is the room where Caroline and her children wait, and where her husband returns home with news of the dead man's death.

nodes.name: Relenting
nodes.description: Relenting refers to the possibility of the dead man changing his harsh stance on debts, which is now impossible due to his death.

nodes.name: Rats
nodes.description: Rats are animals gnawing beneath the hearthstone, symbolizing decay and neglect in the room of the dead man.

nodes.name: Cat
nodes.description: A cat is tearing at the door of the room where the dead man lies, adding to the sense of disturbance and desolation.

nodes.name: Cratchit's Wife
nodes.description: Cratchit's wife is the mother of the Cratchit children and is emotionally affected by the loss of Tiny Tim, as indicated by her reaction to candlelight. | Cratchit's wife is a supportive and resilient mother who manages the household and comforts her family, especially after Bob's emotional outburst.

nodes.name: The Half-Drunk Woman
nodes.description: The half-drunk woman is a minor character who informed Caroline about the dying state of the deceased man, which was initially dismissed as an excuse.

nodes.name: And He Took a Child, and Set Him in the Midst of Them
nodes.description: A biblical passage read aloud by Peter, symbolizing themes of innocence, care, and loss in the context of Tiny Tim's death.
nodes.id: And He Took a Child, and Set Him in the Midst of Them

nodes.name: The Two Young Cratchits
nodes.description: The two young Cratchits are children who comfort their father by placing their cheeks against his face, showing affection and empathy. | The two young Cratchits are children in the Cratchit family who express affection for their father and share in the family's emotional moments.

nodes.name: The Room Above
nodes.description: The room above is a cheerfully lit, Christmas-decorated space where Bob Cratchit goes to grieve and reconcile with the loss of Tiny Tim.

nodes.name: Sunday Walk
nodes.description: The Sunday walk is a promised ritual Bob Cratchit made to visit a green place in memory of Tiny Tim, which he fulfilled on the day described.

nodes.name: Mr. Scrooge's Nephew
nodes.description: Mr. Scrooge's nephew is a kind and compassionate gentleman who showed concern for Bob Cratchit and offered assistance, leaving a strong impression on him.

nodes.name: The Girls
nodes.description: The girls are Bob and Mrs. Cratchit's daughters, who participate in the family conversation and express emotional solidarity with their father.

nodes.name: The Card
nodes.description: The card is given by Mr. Scrooge's nephew to Bob Cratchit, serving as a tangible symbol of his offer of support and kindness.

nodes.name: The Grave
nodes.description: The grave is a physical site in a churchyard where Scrooge is shown the final resting place of a man he ultimately recognizes as himself. | The grave is a neglected burial site marked with Ebenezer Scrooge's name, serving as the pivotal vision that triggers his emotional transformation.

nodes.name: Churchyard
nodes.description: The churchyard is a burial ground surrounded by houses and overgrown with vegetation, symbolizing neglect and the inevitability of death.

nodes.name: The Bedpost
nodes.description: The bedpost is the physical object into which the Ghost collapses, symbolizing the end of the supernatural vision and Scrooge's return to reality.

nodes.name: Christmas Time
nodes.description: Christmas Time represents a period of reflection, generosity, and spiritual renewal that Scrooge resolves to honor throughout the year. | Christmas Time represents a season of joy, redemption, and goodwill that Scrooge embraces after his spiritual experiences.

nodes.name: The Past
nodes.description: The Past is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing memories and lessons from his earlier life.

nodes.name: The Present
nodes.description: The Present is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing his current actions and relationships.

nodes.name: The Future
nodes.description: The Future is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing the potential outcomes of his changed behavior.

nodes.name: The Spirits of All Three
nodes.description: The Spirits of All Three refer to the collective supernatural forces representing Past, Present, and Future, which Scrooge vows to let guide his transformation.

nodes.name: Wandering Spirits
nodes.description: Wandering Spirits are ethereal beings observed by Scrooge through a window, representing the broader realm of supernatural forces influencing his redemption.

nodes.name: Church Bells
nodes.description: Church Bells are physical objects whose ringing signals the passage of time and amplifies Scrooge's sense of joy and renewal.

nodes.name: Gruel Saucepan
nodes.description: The Gruel Saucepan is a household object that Scrooge recognizes as part of his former life, now symbolizing his changed circumstances.

nodes.name: Stockings
nodes.description: Stockings are clothing items that Scrooge mismanages in his ecstatic frenzy, illustrating his emotional upheaval and renewed vitality.

nodes.name: Golden Sunlight
nodes.description: Golden Sunlight is a natural phenomenon Scrooge observes after his transformation, symbolizing clarity, hope, and a renewed world.

nodes.name: Heavenly Sky
nodes.description: Heavenly Sky is the clear, bright sky Scrooge sees upon opening his window, reflecting his inner transformation and joy.

nodes.name: Sweet Fresh Air
nodes.description: Sweet Fresh Air is the crisp, invigorating atmosphere Scrooge experiences after his spiritual journey, signifying renewal and purity.

nodes.name: Prize Turkey
nodes.description: The prize turkey is a large, notable bird hanging at the poulterer's shop, selected by Scrooge to be delivered anonymously to Bob Cratchit's home.

nodes.name: Poulterer's Shop
nodes.description: The poulterer's shop is a retail location in the next street but one, where the prize turkey is displayed and purchased by Scrooge's instructions.

nodes.name: Boy
nodes.description: The boy is a young street urchin in Sunday clothes whom Scrooge engages to purchase the turkey and deliver instructions to the poulterer. | The boy is a young individual whom Scrooge recompenses, likely for helping with the turkey purchase or delivery.

nodes.name: Knocker
nodes.description: The knocker is the door knocker on Scrooge's house, which he suddenly notices and affectionately praises after his transformation.

nodes.name: Turkey
nodes.description: The turkey is a large bird purchased by Scrooge, considered too big to carry to Camden Town without a cab.

nodes.name: Cab
nodes.description: A cab is hired by Scrooge to transport the turkey to Camden Town.

nodes.name: Portly Gentleman
nodes.description: The portly gentleman is a visitor who previously approached Scrooge at his counting house to request a charitable donation, and is now met by Scrooge with unexpected warmth.

nodes.name: Shaving
nodes.description: Shaving is a daily grooming activity that Scrooge performs with difficulty due to his shaking hand, yet he remains determined to complete it.

nodes.name: Sticking-Plaster
nodes.description: A sticking-plaster is a small adhesive bandage Scrooge imagines using if he had accidentally cut his nose while shaving.

nodes.name: Scrooge's Niece by Marriage
nodes.description: Scrooge's niece by marriage is present at Fred's dinner, and is startled when Scrooge enters the dining room unexpectedly.

nodes.name: Christmas Bowl of Smoking Bishop
nodes.description: A festive drink referenced by Scrooge as part of his gesture to celebrate Christmas and discuss Bob Cratchit's affairs.

nodes.name: Coal Scuttle
nodes.description: An object Scrooge instructs Bob Cratchit to purchase, indicating improved working conditions and warmth in the office.

nodes.name: Salary Increase
nodes.description: The decision by Scrooge to raise Bob Cratchit's pay, symbolizing a shift from miserliness to generosity.

nodes.name: Total-Abstinence Principle
nodes.description: The Total-Abstinence Principle is a lifestyle adopted by Scrooge after his transformation, implying he abstains from any form of spiritual or moral excess, particularly related to his past behavior.

Mrs. Cratchit is the wife of Bob Cratchit, who works for Ebenezer Scrooge. | Bob Cratchit and his wife live together in poverty, managing their limited resources to create a festive atmosphere for their children. | Bob and Mrs. Cratchit share a loving interaction as they reflect together on the day’s events and the memory of Tiny Tim. | Mrs. Cratchit kisses Bob Cratchit, demonstrating emotional support and love within the family unit.
The Spirit blesses Bob Cratchit's home, showing kindness and recognition of his dignity despite poverty.
Bob Cratchit gives his son Peter his old shirt collar as a symbolic gift, reflecting familial pride despite economic hardship.
Bob Cratchit earns fifteen shillings per week, highlighting his impoverished economic status despite his hard work and family responsibilities.

Bob Cratchit carries Tiny Tim on his shoulder after returning from church, showing his affection and protective role as a father. | Bob Cratchit holds his daughter close and speaks tenderly about Tiny Tim, expressing pride in his son's growth and thoughtfulness. | Bob Cratchit holds Tiny Tim’s hand affectionately, showing deep emotional attachment and fear of losing him. | Bob Cratchit is the father of Tiny Tim, whose death has caused profound sorrow in the family. | Bob Cratchit deeply loved Tiny Tim and carried him on his shoulder during walks, a memory that brings him both comfort and sorrow. | Bob Cratchit remembers Tiny Tim with deep affection, emphasizing his gentle nature and how his memory unites the family. | Bob Cratchit expresses deep affection for Tiny Tim, his sickly son, highlighting the emotional core of the Cratchit family.
Bob Cratchit wears his monstrous shirt-collar, a family heirloom, with pride on Christmas Day.
Martha hides to surprise Bob, and when she emerges, he embraces her, relieved and delighted by her return on Christmas Day.
Bob Cratchit wears a large comforter to appear appropriately dressed for the holiday, despite his threadbare clothes.
Martha emerges to comfort Bob Cratchit when he appears disappointed, showing her affection and attentiveness.
Bob Cratchit prepares the hot mixture of gin and lemons, a ritual that adds warmth and celebration to the meal.
Bob Cratchit seats Tiny Tim beside the fire, creating a warm, intimate space for his son during the meal.
Bob Cratchit expresses universal admiration for the goose's qualities, declaring it unmatched.
Bob Cratchit praises the pudding as the greatest success of his wife since their marriage.
Bob Cratchit organizes and leads the Christmas dinner, serving food and drink with joy despite poverty.

Bob Cratchit is employed by Scrooge, who is viewed with bitterness by Bob's family despite Bob's mild demeanor. | Scrooge plans to send the prize turkey anonymously to Bob Cratchit's home as an act of kindness. | Scrooge, initially confrontational, surprises Bob Cratchit by raising his salary and expressing goodwill after his Christmas tardiness. | Scrooge, after his transformation, becomes a better master to Bob Cratchit, improving their relationship significantly.

Bob Cratchit speaks of a job opportunity for Peter, expressing hope for his son's future financial stability. | Peter Cratchit shakes hands with his father, symbolizing mutual respect and maturity within the family.
The Ghost shows Scrooge Bob Cratchit's home to reveal the emotional consequences of Tiny Tim's death.
Bob Cratchit promised to walk to a green place on Sundays in memory of Tiny Tim, and he fulfilled this promise on the day described.
Cratchit's wife supports Bob emotionally, maintaining a cheerful tone and rushing to greet him, showing deep familial care.

Peter notices his father's slower walking pace and recalls memories of Tiny Tim, indicating his awareness of his father's grief. | Bob expresses hope that Mr. Scrooge's nephew might help Peter secure a better job, showing his optimism for his son's future.

The two young Cratchits comfort their father by physically touching him, helping him feel loved and less alone in his grief. | The two young Cratchits kiss their father, reinforcing the warmth and closeness of the family despite their poverty.
Bob Cratchit goes to the room above to grieve privately, where he finds comfort in the presence of his child's memory and Christmas decorations.
Bob Cratchit recounts how Mr. Scrooge's nephew showed unexpected kindness by inquiring about his distress and offering help, deeply moving him.
Five-and-sixpence weekly is the projected income for Peter Cratchit's potential job, symbolizing the family's economic hopes.
Tiny Tim relies on the iron frame to support his limbs, indicating his physical condition during the Christmas celebration.
Tiny Tim expresses a hope that people will see him in church on Christmas Day, linking his identity as a cripple to religious symbolism.
Tiny Tim uses the crutch to move, and its sound on the floor marks his return to the family, symbolizing his presence and resilience.
Tiny Tim is taken to the wash-house to shield him from hearing the pudding cooking, indicating the family's thoughtful care.
Tiny Tim reacts with excitement to the serving of the goose, beating the table and cheering.

Scrooge becomes emotionally moved by the vision of Tiny Tim’s potential death, prompting his plea for mercy and change. | Tiny Tim drinks the toast to Scrooge without feeling, reflecting his innocence and detachment from the family's animosity. | Scrooge watches Tiny Tim closely during the family gathering, showing particular attention to him until the end. | Scrooge references Tiny Tim's small size to emphasize the exceptional largeness of the turkey he is sending to Bob Cratchit. | Scrooge becomes a second father to Tiny Tim, demonstrating his newfound compassion and commitment to others' well-being.
Tiny Tim’s final words in the Christmas toast serve as the emotional climax of the celebration, reinforcing themes of hope and innocence.

edges.target: A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow
edges.description: Tiny Tim sings the song about a lost child in the snow, delivering it with a plaintive and skilled voice.
Cratchit's wife is emotionally affected by the death of Tiny Tim, as seen in her reaction to candlelight and tears.
The room above holds signs of Tiny Tim's recent presence, serving as a physical and emotional anchor for Bob's grief and reconciliation.
The entire family pledges never to forget Tiny Tim, using his memory as a bond to prevent future quarrels.

edges.target: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
edges.description: Tiny Tim’s spirit and fate are implicitly linked to the visions shown by the Ghost, serving as a moral counterpoint to Scrooge’s potential end.
Mr. Fezziwig and Mrs. Fezziwig are partners who host festive gatherings, including Christmas dances.

Scrooge visits Fred and asks to be let in for dinner, indicating a potential reconciliation or change in relationship. | Fred, Scrooge's nephew, organizes a celebration and leads a toast to Scrooge's health despite their differences. | Scrooge visits Fred's home for dinner, initiating a reconciliation after prior estrangement.
Ebenezer Scrooge is shown visions of his past by the Ghost of Christmas Past.

edges.target: Stave Two--The First of the Three Spirits
edges.description: Stave Two centers on the appearance and role of the Ghost of Christmas Past in Scrooge's transformation.

Scrooge encounters the Ghost of Christmas Past, who reveals itself as a manifestation of his own past and seeks to guide his reclamation. | The Ghost of Christmas Past physically grasps Scrooge's arm to compel him to rise and accompany it, overriding Scrooge's objections. | The Ghost of Christmas Past shows Scrooge memories from his childhood, particularly associated with the musical tune played on the harp.
Ebenezer Scrooge is shown the current state of others, particularly the Cratchit family, by the Ghost of Christmas Present.

edges.target: Stave Three--The Second of the Three Spirits
edges.description: Stave Three centers on the appearance and role of the Ghost of Christmas Present in Scrooge's transformation.

The Ghost of Christmas Present appears to Scrooge to teach him about the current state of Christmas and the importance of generosity. | Scrooge encountered the Ghost of Christmas Present, whose revelations contributed to his emotional transformation.
The Ghost of Christmas Present holds the glowing torch to shed light on Scrooge, symbolizing the revelation of Christmas's true spirit.
The Ghost of Christmas Present wears a holly wreath adorned with icicles, symbolizing the festive and wintry nature of Christmas.
The Ghost of Christmas Present carries an empty, rusted scabbard, indicating the absence of violence and the peaceful nature of the season.
The Ghost of Christmas Present is surrounded by mince-pies and other festive foods, symbolizing the generosity and richness of the season.
The Ghost of Christmas Present is depicted amid plum-puddings, emphasizing the opulence and tradition of Christmas feasting.
Barrels of oysters surround the Ghost of Christmas Present, representing the lavishness of Christmas celebrations.
Red-hot chestnuts contribute to the steamy, inviting atmosphere of the Ghost of Christmas Present's environment.
Cherry-cheeked apples are part of the abundant fruit display surrounding the Ghost of Christmas Present, symbolizing vitality and festivity.
Juicy oranges are among the luxurious fruits associated with the Ghost of Christmas Present's feast, indicating seasonal wealth.
Luscious pears are part of the rich array of fruits displayed in the Ghost of Christmas Present's domain.
Immense twelfth-cakes are featured in the Ghost of Christmas Present's scene, representing the culmination of Christmas festivities.
Seething bowls of punch contribute to the warm, aromatic, and celebratory atmosphere of the Ghost of Christmas Present's presence.
Holly is used decoratively in the Ghost of Christmas Present's attire and surroundings, symbolizing Christmas traditions.
Mistletoe is among the festive plants decorating the Ghost of Christmas Present's environment, tied to holiday customs.
Red berries, found on holly and mistletoe, enhance the visual richness of the Ghost of Christmas Present's appearance.
Ivy is part of the traditional Christmas greenery that adorns the Ghost of Christmas Present's setting.
Turkeys are among the meats featured in the Christmas feast surrounding the Ghost of Christmas Present.
Geese are part of the traditional Christmas meats displayed in the scene, reflecting seasonal culinary customs.
The Ghost of Christmas Present is implied to know about the collusion between Topper and Scrooge's nephew during the game.
